1.title: Articles with Titles
1.body: Use definite articles (el/la) with titles when describing someone, but omit them when directly addressing the person.
1.tip: Think: Describe WITH article, address WITHOUT article.

1.title: Género de los Sustantivos
1.body: Los sustantivos en español tienen género masculino o femenino. Generalmente, los que terminan en -o son masculinos y los que terminan en -a son femeninos, pero hay excepciones importantes.
1.tip: Recuerda: palabras que terminan en -ción, -sión, -dad, -tad, -tud son siempre femeninas.

1.title: Spanish Vowel Sounds
1.body: Spanish has five simple vowels, each with one consistent spelling and sound, unlike English.
1.tip: Remember AEIOU: each vowel has one clear sound in Spanish.

1.title: Accent Marks for Stress
1.body: The accent mark (á, é, í, ó, ú) shows which syllable gets the loudest stress in pronunciation. It's a guide to saying words correctly.
1.tip: Think of the accent mark as a loudness spotlight on that vowel.

1.title: Spanish Stress Rules
1.body: Words ending in vowel, n, or s stress the second-to-last syllable. Others stress the last syllable. An accent mark is used when stress breaks these rules.
1.tip: Vowel, n, s = penultimate stress; other endings = last syllable stress.

1.title: Contractions al & del
1.body: In Spanish, 'a + el' contracts to 'al' and 'de + el' contracts to 'del'. These are mandatory contractions.

examples.en: The book is the professor's (of the professor).

1.tip: Think 'a+el' and 'de+el' always stick together like glue to become 'al' and 'del'.

1.title: Ser vs Estar
1.body: Ser describes permanent traits like identity and origin. Estar describes temporary states like location and health.
1.tip: Remember: Ser = permanent, Estar = temporary.

1.title: Ser + de = Origin
1.body: Use 'ser' plus 'de' to indicate where someone or something is from.
1.tip: Remember: 'ser de' = 'to be from' (permanent origin)

1.title: Subject Pronouns
1.body: Pronouns replace nouns as verb subjects. In Spanish, they're often omitted because verb endings already show the subject.
1.tip: Use tú with friends, usted with strangers. In Latin America, use ustedes for all 'you plural' situations.

1.title: Ser vs. Estar with Military
1.body: Use 'ser' for stating a military rank or profession. Use 'estar' for stating you are in the Armed Forces or a specific branch.
1.tip: Ser = permanent role, Estar = temporary location/state

1.title: Estar + en para ubicación
1.body: Usa 'estar' con la preposición 'en' para indicar la ubicación física de personas, lugares o cosas.

examples.es: Las alumnas están en la escuela.

1.tip: Recuerda: 'estar' = estado temporal, 'en' = dentro de un lugar

1.title: Hay: There is/are
1.body: Use 'hay' for both singular and plural impersonal expressions meaning 'there is' or 'there are'. It never changes form.

examples.es: Hay un libro en la mesa.
examples.en: There is a book on the table.

1.tip: Remember: 'Hay' = 'There is/are' - one word for both singular and plural!
1.source_label: ¡Atención! Impersonal use of haber (to be): hay

1.title: El vs La: Changing Meanings
1.body: Some Spanish nouns can be masculine or feminine, but their meaning changes with the article.
1.tip: Think: same word, different gender = different meaning!

1.title: Spanish Inverted Punctuation
1.body: Spanish uses inverted question marks (¿) and exclamation points (¡) at the beginning of questions and exclamations, with regular marks at the end.
1.tip: Think of Spanish punctuation as bookends—both opening and closing marks are needed.

1.title: Tener - Present Tense
1.body: Tener is irregular in the present tense. The stem changes to 'tien-' for tú, él/ella/Ud., and ellos/ellas/Uds.
1.tip: Remember 'tengo' for yo, then 'tien-' for most others except nosotros/vosotros.
1.source_label: The Present Tense of tener (to have)

1.title: Accents in Names
1.body: Spanish names get accent marks in Spanish contexts or lists, but not in English sentences. Capital letters are never accented in this course.

examples.es: La Sra. Pérez es de Perú.

1.tip: Think: Spanish context = accent, English context = no accent. Capitals never get accents here.

1.title: Spanish Stress Rules
1.body: Words ending in vowel, n, or s stress the second-to-last syllable. Words ending in other consonants stress the last syllable. Exceptions require a written accent.
1.tip: Vowel, N, S = penultimate stress; other consonants = last syllable stress; accent marks show exceptions.

1.title: Interrogative Words
1.body: Use these question words to ask for information. They always have an accent mark and come at the beginning of questions.
1.tip: Remember: all question words have accent marks (¿qué?, ¿dónde?, ¿cuándo?, etc.)

1.title: Apellidos hispanos
1.body: Los hispanos usan dos apellidos: primero el paterno, luego el materno. Las mujeres casadas pueden añadir 'de' con el apellido del esposo.

examples.en: First name: Juan Carlos, Father's surname: García, Mother's surname: López
María González (maiden name) married to someone with surname Rodríguez

1.tip: Remember: paternal first, maternal second → like parents' order in family

1.title: Cognados Verdaderos/Falsos
1.body: Cognates are words with shared origin in English and Spanish. True cognates have similar spelling and meaning, while false cognates look alike but mean different things.
1.tip: True friends help (true cognates), false friends trick (false cognates)!

1.title: Plural de sustantivos
1.body: Para formar el plural, generalmente añade -s si termina en vocal, -es si termina en consonante, y cambia -z a -c antes de añadir -es.
1.tip: Recuerda: vocal + s, consonante + es, z cambia a c + es.

1.title: Capitalización en español
1.body: En español, no se capitalizan idiomas, nacionalidades, días ni meses como en inglés. Solo nombres propios llevan mayúscula.
1.tip: Recuerda: en español, lo 'común' (idiomas, días, meses) va en minúscula; lo 'propio' (nombres, países) en mayúscula.

1.title: Affirmative Sentences
1.body: In Spanish, the typical word order is subject, then verb, then object (SVO). This is similar to English.
1.tip: Think Subject-Verb-Object, just like English.

1.title: Interrogative Word Order
1.body: In Spanish questions, the subject and verb are often inverted. Place the verb before the subject to form a question.
1.tip: Think: Verb first, subject second in questions.

1.title: SER vs ESTAR
1.body: Both mean 'to be' but SER describes permanent traits (identity, origin) while ESTAR describes temporary states (location, condition).
1.tip: Remember: SER = permanent (like DNA), ESTAR = temporary (like weather).

1.title: Negación con 'no'
1.body: Para formar oraciones negativas, coloca 'no' directamente antes del verbo. Si respondes negativamente a una pregunta, usa 'no' dos veces: al inicio y antes del verbo.

examples.es: ¿Tiene Jaime dinero? No, Jaime no tiene dinero.
examples.en: Does Jaime have money? No, Jaime does not have money.

1.tip: Piensa en 'no' como un imán que siempre se pega al verbo.

2.title: Feminine Nouns with Stressed 'a'
2.body: Feminine nouns starting with stressed 'a' or 'ha' use 'el' in singular but remain feminine, taking feminine adjectives and 'las' in plural.
2.tip: Think: 'el' for singular, 'las' for plural—like a temporary masculine disguise!
2.source_label: Grammar Note: Feminine Nouns with Stressed 'a' or 'ha'

2.title: Verb Endings
2.body: Spanish verbs have three infinitive endings: -ar, -er, -ir. The ending changes when conjugated for different subjects and tenses.

examples.en: to speak, to eat, to live

examples.es: Yo hablo, tú comes, él vive
examples.en: I speak, you eat, he lives

2.title: Plural de Sustantivos
2.body: Para formar el plural: añade -s si termina en vocal, -es si termina en consonante, y cambia -z a -c antes de añadir -es.
2.tip: Zorro cambia a zorros, pero lápiz cambia a lápices. ¡La Z se vuelve C antes de ES!
2.source_label: Grammar Note: Plural of Nouns Rules

2.title: Article Changes Meaning
2.body: Some Spanish nouns change meaning completely depending on whether they use 'el' (masculine) or 'la' (feminine).
2.tip: Remember: 'el Papa' is holy, 'la papa' is humble (potato).
2.source_label: Grammar Note: Nouns Changing Meaning with Article

2.title: Tener for Age
2.body: Use 'tener' (to have) with years to express age in Spanish, not 'ser' (to be).

examples.es: Ella tiene treinta y cinco años.

2.tip: Think 'I have years' not 'I am years'.

2.title: Familia Extendida
2.body: In Spanish-speaking cultures, 'la familia' typically refers to the extended family, including grandparents, aunts, uncles, and cousins, not just the nuclear family.

examples.es: Los domingos, toda la familia se reúne para comer.
examples.en: On Sundays, the whole extended family gathers to eat.

examples.es: Mis parientes viven en diferentes ciudades.
examples.en: My relatives live in different cities.

2.tip: Think 'familia' = family tree, not just parents and kids.

2.title: Ser vs. Estar
2.body: Use 'ser' for permanent traits like identity and character, and 'estar' for temporary states and locations.
2.tip: Remember: 'ser' is for 'D.O.C.T.O.R.' (Description, Occupation, Characteristic, Time, Origin, Relationship), 'estar' is for 'P.L.A.C.E.' (Position, Location, Action, Condition, Emotion).

2.title: Con = With
2.body: Use 'con' to indicate accompaniment, means, or manner. It connects people, tools, or methods.

2.title: Expressing Age
2.body: Use 'tener' (to have) with 'años' to express age, not 'ser' (to be).
2.tip: Think 'I have years' not 'I am years'.

2.title: Y vs E, O vs U
2.body: Use 'e' instead of 'y' before words starting with i/hi (except hie). Use 'u' instead of 'o' before words starting with o/ho.
2.tip: Change y/e and o/u to avoid double vowel sounds.

3.title: Tú vs Usted
3.body: Use tú with friends, family, and peers. Use usted with strangers, elders, and in formal situations to show respect.
3.tip: Think: tú = 'too' familiar, usted = 'usted' better be polite!
3.source_label: Grammar Note: Tú vs. Usted Usage

3.title: Double Negative in Spanish
3.body: In Spanish, use 'no' twice for negative answers: once before the verb and once before the negative element.

examples.en: No, Jaime does not have money.

examples.es: No, no voy nunca al cine.
examples.en: No, I never go to the movies.

3.tip: Think: 'No, I don't...' becomes 'No, no...' in Spanish.

3.title: Subject Pronouns
3.body: Spanish has different 'you' forms based on formality and number. Use tú for one familiar person, usted for one formal person, vosotros/vosotras for familiar groups, and ustedes for formal groups.
3.tip: Think: tú = 'you' to a friend, usted = 'you' to a boss or elder.

3.title: Long Possessive Adjectives
3.body: Long possessive adjectives agree in gender and number with the noun they modify and follow the noun, often with articles.
3.tip: Think 'of mine/yours' - they come after the noun, unlike short forms.

3.title: Two 'To Be' Verbs
3.body: Spanish has two main verbs for 'to be': SER for permanent traits and ESTAR for temporary states. They are not interchangeable.
3.tip: Remember: SER = permanent (like DNA), ESTAR = temporary (like mood).

3.title: Ser for Professions
3.body: Use ser with professions, nationalities, and origins. It describes permanent characteristics.
3.tip: Remember DOCTOR: Description, Occupation, Characteristic, Time, Origin, Relationship.

3.title: Origin with 'de'
3.body: Use 'ser + de' to say where someone or something is from. It indicates origin or nationality.
3.tip: Think: 'ser de' = 'to be from' — just add the place after 'de'.

3.title: En for Location
3.body: Use 'en' with 'estar' to indicate location. It translates to 'in', 'at', or 'on' depending on context.

examples.es: Las alumnas están en la escuela.

3.tip: Think: estar + en = to be located in/at

3.title: Estar for Location & State
3.body: Estar is used for temporary conditions and locations. It describes how or where someone/something is at a specific moment.
3.tip: Remember PLACE: Position, Location, Action, Condition, Emotion.

3.title: Qué vs Cuál
3.body: Use qué for definitions and general information. Use cuál for specific choices or identifying particular items from known options.

examples.en: What is the capital of Chile?

3.tip: Qué = 'What is it?' (definition). Cuál = 'Which one?' (selection).

3.title: Cuál vs Qué
3.body: Use 'qué' for definitions and 'cuál' for choices. 'Qué' asks 'what is it?' while 'cuál' asks 'which one?'
3.tip: Remember: Qué = definition, Cuál = selection

3.title: Ser vs Estar
3.body: Ser describes permanent traits like identity and origin. Estar describes temporary states like location and condition.
3.tip: Use DOCTOR for Ser (Description, Occupation, Characteristic, Time, Origin, Relationship) and PLACE for Estar (Position, Location, Action, Condition, Emotion).

3.title: Verbs with Built-in 'For'
3.body: Verbs like buscar, esperar, and pedir already contain 'for' in their meaning, so they are not followed by por or para.
3.tip: Think: 'for' is already inside these verbs—no extra por/para needed!

3.title: Reflexive vs Non-Reflexive Verbs
3.body: Reflexive verbs (sentarse, reírse) use reflexive pronouns and indicate actions done to oneself. Non-reflexive verbs (sentir, reír) don't use reflexive pronouns.

examples.en: I sit myself in the chair.

3.tip: If you can add 'myself/yourself' in English, it's probably reflexive in Spanish.

3.title: Su vs. de + pronoun
3.body: Use 'su/sus' for his/her/your/their, but clarify with 'de + pronoun' when context is unclear.
3.tip: Think: 'su' is ambiguous; add 'de + pronoun' to specify.
3.source_label: More about the short forms of the possessive adjectives

3.title: De for Possession
3.body: Use 'de' to show possession or relationship, not an apostrophe like in English. When 'de' meets 'el', they combine to form 'del'.
3.tip: Think 'of' instead of 's': Juan's table = the table of Juan.
3.source_label: Another use of the preposition de

3.title: Hay que + infinitivo
3.body: Se usa 'hay que' para expresar obligación general o necesidad impersonal, sin especificar quién debe hacer la acción.

examples.es: Hay que estudiar para el examen.
examples.en: One must study for the exam.

3.tip: Piensa en 'hay que' como 'it's necessary to' en inglés - ambos son impersonales.

3.title: Numbers to 5 Million
3.body: Spanish uses periods for thousands and commas for decimals, opposite of English. For large numbers, say 'millones' for millions.
3.tip: Think: Spanish periods are 'thousand separators', commas are 'decimal points'.

3.title: Days of the Week: Articles
3.body: Omit the article when stating today's day (Hoy es...). Use 'el' for future/past events (La fiesta es el lunes).
3.tip: Think: 'Today is...' = no article. 'On Monday...' = needs 'el'.

3.title: Adjective Placement
3.body: Most Spanish adjectives go after the noun, but some change form or meaning when placed before.
3.tip: Think: 'description follows' for most adjectives.

3.title: Hacer - Present Tense
3.body: Hacer is irregular in the present tense. The yo form changes to 'hago', while other forms follow regular -er patterns with stem 'hac-'.
3.tip: Remember 'I do' is 'hago' - think 'I go do it' to link 'hago' with action.
3.source_label: ¡Atención! The Present Tense of hacer (to do, to make)

3.title: Posesivos largos
3.body: Los posesivos largos (mío, tuyo, suyo, etc.) van después del sustantivo y concuerdan en género y número. Añaden énfasis o contraste.
3.tip: Recuerda: 'mío' sigue al sustantivo como 'amigo mío' (my friend).
3.source_label: More about the long forms of the possessive adjectives

3.title: Cien vs. Ciento
3.body: Use 'cien' before nouns and when counting by hundreds. Use 'ciento' for numbers 101-199.
3.tip: Think: 'cien' is complete (100 exactly), 'ciento' needs more (101+).

3.title: Time Prepositions: a vs. por/en
3.body: Use 'a' with specific times, and 'por' or 'en' for general periods without a specific hour.

examples.es: A las ocho de la mañana

3.tip: Remember: 'a' for 'at' a time, 'por/en' for 'in' a period.

3.title: Es vs. Son for Time
3.body: Use 'es la' only for 1:00. Use 'son las' for all other hours. The hour is feminine, so always use 'la' or 'las'.
3.tip: Think: 'es' is singular for 1, 'son' is plural for 2+.

3.title: Adjetivos de nacionalidad
3.body: Adjectives of nationality ending in a consonant add -a for feminine form. Others like español/española follow this rule.
3.tip: Think: consonant-ending nationalities get an 'a' for feminine - just like adding 'a' to señor to make señora.

3.title: Formato de números y fechas
3.body: En español, los números grandes y las fechas siguen formatos distintos al inglés. Se usan puntos para miles y comas para decimales, y las fechas se escriben día-mes-año.
3.tip: Recuerda: día primero, luego mes, luego año — como orden natural de pequeño a grande.

3.title: Pronombres Posesivos
3.body: Reemplazan un sustantivo para mostrar posesión. Usan artículo definido + adjetivo posesivo largo, sin el sustantivo.

examples.es: Mi coche es rojo. El tuyo es azul.
examples.en: My car is red. Yours is blue.

examples.es: Nuestra casa es grande. La suya es pequeña.
examples.en: Our house is big. Theirs is small.

3.tip: Mío/tuyo/suyo = mine/yours/theirs. Match gender/number to the item possessed, not the owner.

3.title: Adjetivos plurales
3.body: Los adjetivos deben concordar en número con el sustantivo que describen. Para formar el plural, añade -s si termina en vocal, -es si termina en consonante, y cambia -z a -c antes de añadir -es.

examples.es: La casa hermosa / Las casas hermosas
examples.en: The beautiful house / The beautiful houses

examples.es: El libro popular / Los libros populares
examples.en: The popular book / The popular books

examples.es: Un niño feliz / Unos niños felices
examples.en: A happy child / Some happy children

3.tip: Recuerda: vocal + S, consonante + ES, Z cambia a C + ES.

4.title: Adjective Position
4.body: Most Spanish adjectives go after the noun. Some change form or meaning when placed before the noun.
4.tip: Think: 'house big' not 'big house' for most cases.

4.title: Su/Sus Clarification
4.body: Su/sus can mean his, her, your, or their. To clarify, use 'de + pronoun' before the noun.
4.tip: Think: 'su' is ambiguous, add 'de + pronoun' to specify.
4.source_label: More about the short forms of the possessive adjectives

4.title: Motion Verbs + a
4.body: Use 'a' after motion verbs like ir, venir, subir, bajar to show direction toward a place or person.
4.tip: Think: Motion + a = moving toward something.
4.source_label: The preposition a (to) follows a verb of motion

4.title: De for Material
4.body: Use 'de' to indicate what something is made of, connecting the object and its material.
4.tip: Think 'de' = 'made of' or 'of' when describing materials.

4.title: De for Possession
4.body: Use 'de' to show possession or relationship, not apostrophes like in English. When 'de' meets 'el', they combine to form 'del'.
4.tip: Think 'of' instead of 's': Juan's table = the table of Juan.
4.source_label: Another use of the preposition de

4.title: Tener que + infinitive
4.body: Use 'tener que' + infinitive to express obligation or necessity. It means 'to have to' do something.

examples.en: We have to make the coffee.

4.tip: Think: 'I have TO' → 'Tengo QUE'

4.title: Más/Menos de + Number
4.body: Use 'más de' or 'menos de' with numbers to express 'more than' or 'less than' a specific quantity.

examples.en: I have more than twenty books.

examples.es: Ella trabaja menos de ocho horas.
examples.en: She works less than eight hours.

4.tip: Think 'de' for definite numbers, 'que' for comparisons between things.

4.title: Periphrastic Future
4.body: Use 'ir a + infinitive' to talk about future plans, similar to 'going to' in English.

examples.en: I am going to study Spanish.
What are you going to do tomorrow?

4.tip: Think 'I'm going to...' = 'Voy a...'
4.source_label: Talking about future plans: periphrastic future

4.title: Ir - Present Tense
4.body: Ir is irregular in the present tense. It uses unique forms that don't follow regular patterns.
4.tip: Remember 'voy, vas, va, vamos, vais, van' - it sounds like 'boy, boss, bah, bah-mos, bye-s, bahn'
4.source_label: The Present Tense of ir (to go)

4.title: Short Possessive Adjectives
4.body: Short possessive adjectives come before nouns and agree in number (singular/plural) with the thing possessed, not the gender. Mi, tu, and su have only singular/plural forms.
4.tip: Think 'short before' - short forms go before the noun and match its number.

4.title: Más/Menos...que
4.body: Use 'más...que' for 'more than' and 'menos...que' for 'less than' when comparing inequality between two things.

examples.es: El sofá cuesta más dinero que la silla.
examples.en: The sofa costs more money than the chair.

examples.es: El baño está menos limpio que la cocina.
examples.en: The bathroom is less clean than the kitchen.

4.tip: Think: 'más' = plus sign (+) for more, 'menos' = minus sign (-) for less.

4.title: Contracciones: al y del
4.body: Cuando 'a' o 'de' van antes de 'el', se combinan en 'al' y 'del'. No ocurre con otros artículos como 'la', 'los', 'las'.
4.tip: Recuerda: 'a el' y 'de el' siempre se contraen. Solo con 'el', nunca con 'la'.

4.title: Adjetivos: Plural
4.body: Make adjectives plural by adding -s if they end in a vowel, or -es if they end in a consonant. Change -z to -c before adding -es.
4.tip: Vowel + S, Consonant + ES. Z becomes C first.

4.title: Hay: There is/are
4.body: Use 'hay' for 'there is' or 'there are' in Spanish. It's impersonal and doesn't change with number.

examples.es: Hay un libro en la mesa.
examples.en: There is a book on the table.

4.tip: Think 'hay' = 'there is/are' - one word for both singular and plural.
4.source_label: Impersonal use of haber (to be): hay

4.title: Reflexive Verbs
4.body: Reflexive verbs show the subject does the action to themselves. Use reflexive pronouns like 'me', 'te', 'se' before the verb.

examples.es: Ella se baña con agua fría.

4.tip: Think 'self' - the action reflects back on the subject.

4.title: Ser vs Estar: Permanent vs Temporary
4.body: Use 'ser' for permanent characteristics and 'estar' for temporary states or locations. 'Ser' describes what something is, 'estar' describes how or where something is.

examples.en: The windows are made of aluminum.

4.tip: Remember: SER = D.O.C.T.O.R. (Description, Occupation, Characteristic, Time, Origin, Relationship), ESTAR = P.L.A.C.E. (Position, Location, Action, Condition, Emotion)

4.title: De for Material
4.body: Use 'de' to describe what something is made of, similar to 'of' or 'made of' in English.
4.tip: Think: 'table OF wood' → 'mesa DE madera'

4.title: Ir - Present Tense
4.body: Ir is irregular in present tense. Use voy, vas, va, vamos, vais, van instead of regular -ir endings.
4.tip: Remember: voy, vas, va, vamos, vais, van - all start with 'v' except nosotros (vamos).
4.source_label: ESTRUCTURAS ¡Atención! The Present Tense of ir (to go)

4.title: Contracciones: al y del
4.body: Cuando las preposiciones 'a' o 'de' van antes del artículo 'el', se combinan para formar 'al' y 'del' respectivamente.
4.tip: Recuerda: 'a el' y 'de el' no existen, siempre se contraen.

4.title: Más/Menos que
4.body: Use 'más...que' for 'more...than' and 'menos...que' for 'less...than' in unequal comparisons.

examples.es: El sofá cuesta más dinero que la silla.
examples.en: The sofa costs more money than the chair.

examples.es: El baño está menos limpio que la cocina.
examples.en: The bathroom is less clean than the kitchen.

4.tip: Más = more, menos = less. Both always use 'que' for 'than'.

4.title: Tener que + infinitive
4.body: Use 'tener que' + infinitive to express obligation or necessity. It literally means 'to have to' do something.

examples.es: Tengo que estudiar para el examen.
examples.en: I have to study for the exam.

4.tip: Think of it as 'I have a task to do' - the 'que' connects the obligation to the action.

4.title: Direcciones hispanas
4.body: En direcciones hispanas, el nombre de la calle va primero, seguido del número, piso y apartamento. Además, la 'planta baja' equivale al 'first floor' estadounidense.

examples.es: Avenida 9 de Julio n° 500, planta baja

4.tip: Recuerda: Calle → Número → Piso → Apartamento (orden natural descendente)

5.title: Expressing Age
5.body: Use 'tener' (to have) with 'años' to express age, not 'ser' (to be).

examples.en: The soldier is eighteen years old.

5.tip: Think: 'I have years' not 'I am years'.

5.title: Es vs. Son for Time
5.body: Use 'es la' only for 1:00. Use 'son las' for all other hours. Hours are feminine, so always use 'la' or 'las'.
5.tip: Remember: 'son' for plural hours (2+), 'es' for singular (1).

5.title: Hay que + infinitivo
5.body: Impersonal expression meaning 'it is necessary to' or 'one must'. Always use 'hay' (from haber) + 'que' + infinitive verb.

examples.es: Hay que estudiar todos los días.

5.tip: Think: 'There is that to do...' → Hay que hacer algo.
5.source_label: Atención! Impersonal construction with haber que + infinitive

5.title: Preposición: Con
5.body: La preposición 'con' significa 'with' en inglés y se usa para indicar compañía, instrumento o manera.
5.tip: Think 'con' = 'with' - both start with consonants and have similar sounds.

5.title: Possessive Pronouns
5.body: Replace nouns to show ownership, matching gender/number of the item owned, not the owner.

examples.es: Mi coche es rojo; el tuyo es azul.
examples.en: My car is red; yours is blue.

examples.es: Nuestra casa es grande; la suya es pequeña.
examples.en: Our house is big; theirs is small.

5.tip: Think: 'the mine, the yours' (el mío, el tuyo) – they need the article!

5.title: Posesivos largos
5.body: Los posesivos largos (mío, tuyo, suyo, etc.) van después del sustantivo y concuerdan en género y número. Añaden énfasis o contraste.
5.tip: Recuerda: 'mío' viene después como un eco posesivo del sustantivo.
5.source_label: ¡Atención! More about the long forms of the possessive adjectives

5.title: Y/E, O/U Changes
5.body: Use 'e' instead of 'y' before words starting with i/hi (except hie). Use 'u' instead of 'o' before words starting with o/ho.

5.title: Demonstrative Pronouns
5.body: Demonstrative pronouns point to specific things based on distance: este (this), ese (that), aquel (that over there). They agree in gender and number with the noun they replace.

examples.en: I prefer that one over there.

5.tip: Remember the distance: este (close), ese (medium), aquel (far).

5.title: Regional Variation in Spanish
5.body: Spanish has regional differences in pronunciation and vocabulary, similar to English dialects. These variations are neutral and equally acceptable.
5.tip: Think of it like 'soda' vs 'pop' in English - same meaning, different regions.

5.title: Ir a + Infinitive
5.body: Use 'ir a' + infinitive to express future plans, similar to 'going to' in English. It's a common way to talk about immediate or intended future actions.

examples.en: I am going to wash the clothes.
What are you going to do?

5.tip: Think 'I'm going to...' in English - it works the same way in Spanish with 'ir a' + verb.

6.title: Time Prepositions: A vs. De
6.body: Use 'a' for specific clock times and 'de' when adding morning/afternoon/night. Combine them as 'a las [time] de la [period]'.

examples.es: A las ocho de la mañana

6.tip: Remember: A for At, De for Day part

6.title: Costar & Llover - Impersonal
6.body: Costar and llover are often used impersonally in third person singular/plural (it/they), not referring to a specific subject.
6.tip: Think 'it costs' and 'it rains' - the 'it' isn't a person, just a general statement.
6.source_label: NOTE: The verb costar and llover

6.title: Uno → Un/Una
6.body: Uno shortens to 'un' before masculine nouns and 'una' before feminine nouns. When alone, use 'uno' or 'una'.
6.tip: Think: Uno drops its 'o' before nouns, matching their gender.

6.title: Un/Una: Article or Number
6.body: Un and una can be either indefinite articles (a/an) or numbers (one), depending on context and emphasis.

examples.en: I need a/one pair of shoes.
I want one apple, not two.

6.tip: If you can stress 'one' in English, use un/una as a number; otherwise it's an article.
6.source_label: NOTE: The forms un and una

6.title: Querer + a = to love
6.body: When 'querer' is followed by the preposition 'a', it means 'to love' rather than 'to want'. This is used for people, pets, or things with personal significance.
6.tip: Think of 'a' as pointing to a specific person/thing you love, like an arrow of affection.
6.source_label: NOTE: Querer followed by the preposition a

6.title: Poder, Querer, Necesitar + Infinitivo
6.body: These verbs express ability, desire, or necessity when followed by an infinitive. Conjugate the first verb, then add the unchanged second verb.
6.tip: Think: 'I can/want/need TO DO' something. The second verb always stays in its 'to' form.
6.source_label: Using the conjugated forms of the verbs poder, querer, and necesitar + infinitive

6.title: Spanish Word Stress Patterns
6.body: Spanish words typically stress the last or second-last syllable. Some exceptions stress the third-last or even fourth-last syllable.
6.tip: Think 'dad' vs 'potato' - same letters, different stress changes meaning!

6.title: Question Words vs Intonation
6.body: In Spanish, questions with question words (qué, dónde, etc.) use the same intonation as statements. The question word at the beginning signals it's a question.
6.tip: Listen for words like qué, dónde, cuándo at the start - they mark questions even without rising intonation.

6.title: Complemento Indirecto
6.body: Answers 'to/for whom/what?' and often uses pronouns like le/les. It can indicate recipients or sources of actions.

examples.es: Le escribo una carta a María.
examples.en: I write a letter to María.

examples.es: Les compré regalos a mis padres.
examples.en: I bought gifts for my parents.

6.tip: Think 'to/for' - if you can add these words before the person/thing, it's likely an indirect object.

6.title: Pronombres Personales
6.body: Los pronombres cambian según su función: sujeto, objeto directo, indirecto, o después de preposiciones. Cada persona tiene formas distintas.
6.tip: Recuerda: 'me, te, nos, os' son iguales para directo e indirecto; 'le/les' cambian a 'se' antes de 'lo, la, los, las'.
6.source_label: TABLA DE LOS PRONOMBRES PERSONALES EN FUNCION DE SUJETO DESPUES DE PREPOSICIONES EN FUNCION DE COMPLEMENTO INDIRECTO EN FUNCION DE COMPLEMENTO DIRECTO CON VERBOS REFLEXIVOS

6.title: Uno → Un/Una
6.body: Uno shortens to 'un' before masculine nouns and 'una' before feminine nouns. These forms match the indefinite articles.
6.tip: Think: Uno drops the 'o' before nouns, just like 'bueno' becomes 'buen'.

6.title: POR vs PARA: Exchange vs Purpose
6.body: Use POR for exchanges or rates, PARA for destinations or recipients. Both often translate to 'for' in English but have distinct uses.

6.title: Por vs. Para: Por favor / Gracias por
6.body: Use 'por' after 'gracias' to express thanks for something. 'Por favor' is a fixed phrase meaning 'please'.
6.tip: Remember: Gracias POR something, but 'por favor' is always together.
6.source_label: Grammar Note: Por favor / Gracias por

6.title: Impersonal Verbs
6.body: Impersonal verbs only use third person forms (él/ella/ello, ellos/ellas) because they describe general situations without a specific subject.

examples.en: It costs a lot of money.

6.tip: Think 'it' verbs - if the subject is always 'it' in English, it's probably impersonal in Spanish.

6.title: Cultural Note: Currency Names
6.body: Many Latin American currencies are named after historical figures, indigenous terms, or cultural symbols rather than using generic names like 'dollar'.

examples.es: El colón de Costa Rica honra a Cristóbal Colón.
examples.en: Costa Rica's colón honors Christopher Columbus.

examples.es: El quetzal de Guatemala viene de un ave sagrada maya.
examples.en: Guatemala's quetzal comes from a sacred Mayan bird.

6.tip: Remember: Currency names often tell a story about the country's history or culture.

6.title: Asking for Price
6.body: Use 'cuánto' for singular items and 'cuántos/cuántas' for plural. 'Vale', 'cuesta', and 'es' are all common verbs for asking price.
6.tip: Remember: 'cuánto' = how much, 'cuántos/cuántas' = how many (for plural items)

6.title: Poder, Querer, Necesitar + Infinitivo
6.body: These verbs express ability, desire, or necessity when followed by an infinitive. Conjugate the first verb, then add the second verb in its base form.
6.tip: Think 'helper verb + action verb' - the helper changes, the action stays in infinitive.
6.source_label: ¡Atención! Using the conjugated forms of the verbs poder, querer, and necesitar + infinitive

7.title: Pedir vs. Preguntar
7.body: Pedir means to ask for or request something. Preguntar means to ask a question or inquire about information.
7.tip: Remember: Pedir = request (both start with 'p'), Preguntar = question (both contain 'q').
7.source_label: The verbs pedir and preguntar (to ask)

7.title: POR vs PARA: Exchange vs Purpose
7.body: Use POR for exchanges or rates (in exchange for). Use PARA for purpose or destination (intended for).

7.title: Asking Price
7.body: Use valer, costar, or ser with ¿Cuánto? to ask prices. Match verb number with item(s).
7.tip: Think: 'costar' = to cost, 'valer' = to be worth', 'ser' = to be (total).
7.source_label: To ask the price of something

7.title: Siempre vs Nunca Placement
7.body: Siempre can go before or after the verb. Nunca usually goes before the verb, or after with a double negative (no... nunca).

examples.en: It never rains in Lima. (double negative)

7.tip: Think: 'Always' is flexible, 'never' needs a 'no' buddy when it follows the verb.

7.title: Futuro Simple vs. Ir a + infinitivo
7.body: El futuro simple (estudiaré) expresa mayor compromiso o formalidad, mientras 'ir a + infinitivo' (voy a estudiar) es más informal y común en conversación.

examples.en: Tomorrow I will study for the exam.

examples.es: Mañana voy a estudiar para el examen.
examples.en: Tomorrow I am going to study for the exam.

7.tip: Usa el futuro simple para promesas o planes formales; 'ir a' para planes informales o inmediatos.

7.title: Hace vs. Está for Weather
7.body: Use 'hace' for general weather conditions (hot, cold, sunny). Use 'está' for temporary states (clear, hailing) or with present participles.
7.tip: Hace = how it feels (hot/cold). Está = how it looks (clear/raining).

7.title: Personal 'a'
7.body: Use 'a' before a direct object when it's a specific person or personified thing. This 'personal a' doesn't translate to English.
7.tip: Think: 'a' for animate/human objects, no 'a' for things.

7.title: División política hispana
7.body: Los países hispanohablantes usan diferentes términos para sus divisiones políticas: provincias, departamentos, regiones, estados, municipios y autonomías según el país.

examples.es: México se divide en estados, mientras que España tiene autonomías.
examples.en: Mexico is divided into states, while Spain has autonomies.

examples.es: Argentina y Cuba están organizados en provincias.
examples.en: Argentina and Cuba are organized into provinces.

7.tip: Recuerda: 'provincias' para países como Argentina, 'departamentos' para Colombia y Perú, y 'estados' solo para México y Venezuela.

7.title: Presente progresivo
7.body: Describes actions happening right now. Formed with estar (present) + present participle (-ando/-iendo).
7.tip: Think 'estar + -ing' like English, but use it only for actions in progress now.

7.title: e→i Stem Changes
7.body: Some -ir verbs change e to i in present tense, except in nosotros and vosotros forms.
7.tip: Remember: 'e' becomes 'i' for everyone except 'we' and 'you all'.
7.source_label: ¡Atención! The Present Tense of e⇒ i Stem-changing Verbs

7.title: Hace vs Está for Weather
7.body: Use 'hace' for general weather conditions (hot, cold, sunny). Use 'está' for temporary states (clear, hailing) and verbs for precipitation (llueve, nieva).
7.tip: Hace = how it feels (hot/cold), Está = what it's doing (raining/clear)

7.title: Irregular Future Stems
7.body: Irregular future verbs keep regular endings but change their stem. Memorize these stems, then add -é, -ás, -á, -emos, -éis, -án.

examples.en: She will be able to come tomorrow.

7.tip: Think: 'What will I say? I'll say it with dir-, har-, podr-, etc.'

8.title: Reflexive Verbs
8.body: Reflexive verbs show the subject does the action to themselves. Use reflexive pronouns (me, te, se, nos, os, se) before the verb.
8.tip: Think 'self' action: if the subject does it to themselves, it's reflexive.

8.title: Present Progressive
8.body: Describes actions happening right now. Formed with estar (present tense) + present participle (-ando/-iendo).
8.tip: Think 'estar + -ing' like English, but use -ando for -ar verbs and -iendo for -er/-ir verbs.
8.source_label: Introduction to the present progressive: expressing actions in progress in the present

8.title: Reflexive Pronouns with Body Parts
8.body: When using reflexive verbs with body parts, use definite articles (el, la, los, las) instead of possessive adjectives. The reflexive pronoun already shows ownership.
8.tip: Think: 'I wash the hands' not 'my hands' - the reflexive 'me' already tells you whose hands.

8.title: Haber → Habrá
8.body: The impersonal form 'hay' (there is/are) becomes 'habrá' in the future tense, meaning 'there will be'.
8.tip: Remember: 'hay' today → 'habrá' tomorrow. Same irregular pattern works for conditional tense too.
8.source_label: The future tense - Irregular verbs (Note on haber)

8.title: Reflexive Verb Meaning Changes
8.body: Some Spanish verbs change meaning when used with reflexive pronouns. The reflexive form often indicates the subject performs the action on themselves.

examples.en: He goes to bed at ten.

8.tip: Think: 'se' often means 'self' - dressing yourself, putting yourself to bed.

8.title: Posición de Pronombres Reflexivos
8.body: Los pronombres reflexivos pueden ir antes del verbo conjugado o unidos al infinitivo/gerundio. Al unirse, se añade acento si cambia la sílaba tónica.

examples.es: Me voy a lavar / Voy a lavarme

8.tip: Piensa: 'Me puedo mover' - el pronombre se mueve entre dos posiciones válidas.
8.source_label: ¡Atención! More about the Reflexive Constructions: Position of Reflexive Pronouns in Relation to the Verbs

8.title: Dental Fricative /ð/
8.body: In Spanish, the letter 'd' between vowels or at the end of a word is pronounced as a soft 'th' sound like in 'the'.
8.tip: Think 'd' becomes gentle between vowels - like 'the' in English.

8.title: B/V Pronunciation Rules
8.body: In Spanish, 'b' and 'v' are pronounced the same. After vowels, they sound like English 'v' (written as '¥' in phonetics). After consonants like 's' or 'r', they sound like English 'b'.
8.tip: Think: vowels make B/V softer (like V), consonants make them harder (like B).

8.title: Pronoun Placement
8.body: Object pronouns go before conjugated verbs, but attach to infinitives, gerunds, and affirmative commands.

examples.es: No te olvides de reservarnos las entradas.
examples.en: Don't forget to reserve us the tickets.

8.tip: Think: 'Before the verb, unless it's an -ar, -er, -ir, -ando, -iendo, or ¡Command!'

8.title: Pronombres dobles: orden y cambios
8.body: Cuando usas pronombres de complemento directo e indirecto juntos, el indirecto va primero. Además, 'le' y 'les' cambian a 'se' antes de 'lo, la, los, las'.
8.tip: Orden: primero a QUIÉN (indirecto), luego QUÉ (directo). Recuerda: le/les + lo/la → se lo/la.
8.source_label: 3. LOS PRONOMBRES DE COMPLEMENTO DIRECTO E INDIRECTO USADOS JUNTOS

8.title: Personal 'a'
8.body: Use 'a' before a specific person or pet when they are the direct object of the verb.

8.title: Infinitivo después de preposiciones
8.body: Las preposiciones 'antes de' y 'después de' siempre van seguidas del infinitivo, no del gerundio como en inglés.
8.source_label: ¡Atención! Use of the Infinitive after Prepositions

8.title: Negative Reflexive
8.body: Place 'no' before the reflexive pronoun in simple sentences. With infinitive constructions, 'no' can go before the pronoun or before the conjugated verb.

examples.es: No te vas a lavar el pelo.
examples.en: You are not going to wash your hair.

8.tip: Think: 'no' always comes before what it negates - either the pronoun or the whole verb phrase.

8.title: Ponerse = to become
8.body: Use 'ponerse + adjective' to express becoming/getting into a temporary physical or emotional state.

examples.es: Flora se pone nerviosa en los exámenes.
Él se pondrá triste con la noticia.
examples.en: He will become sad when he hears the news.

8.tip: Think: 'putting yourself' into a state → ponerse + adjective.

8.title: Reflexive Constructions
8.body: Reflexive pronouns (me, te, se, nos, os, se) show that the subject performs the action on themselves. They usually come before the conjugated verb.
8.tip: Think 'self' action: if the subject does it to themselves, add the matching reflexive pronoun.

8.title: Ponerse Future Tense
8.body: Ponerse is irregular in the future tense, following the same pattern as poner. Drop the -er ending and add -dré, -drás, etc.

examples.es: Yo me pondré el uniforme mañana.
examples.en: I will put on my uniform tomorrow.

examples.es: Ella se pondrá el abrigo cuando haga frío.
examples.en: She will put on her coat when it gets cold.

8.tip: Remember: poner → pondré, so ponerse → me pondré
8.source_label: Grammar Note: Ponerse Irregularity in Future Tense

9.title: Forming -mente Adverbs
9.body: Add -mente to feminine adjective forms. Keep original accent marks. These adverbs are invariable.
9.tip: Think: feminine adjective + mente = English -ly equivalent
9.source_label: Culmulative Grammar Notes: More about Adverbs

9.title: solo vs solamente/sólo
9.body: Solo (adjective) means 'alone' and changes with gender/number. Solamente/sólo (adverb) means 'only' and doesn't change.
9.tip: Remember: solo = alone (adjective changes), sólo = only (adverb stays same)
9.source_label: Culmulative Grammar Notes: Adjective solo vs. Adverb solamente/sólo

9.title: Demonstrative Pronouns
9.body: These pronouns point to people/objects and show distance. They agree in gender/number with what they refer to (except neuter forms).
9.tip: Add an accent mark to differentiate from adjectives (except esto/eso/aquello).

9.title: Common Adverbs (not -mente)
9.body: Many useful Spanish adverbs don't end in -mente. These include words for quantity, agreement, and location.
9.tip: Think: 'también' for 'me too', 'tampoco' for 'me neither'.

9.title: Neuter Forms: Esto, Eso, Aquello
9.body: Use esto, eso, and aquello for abstract ideas, general situations, or unspecified things—not specific objects. They translate to 'this/that (thing/matter).'
9.tip: Think 'this stuff' or 'that situation'—neuter forms point to ideas, not items.

9.title: Demonstrative Pronouns
9.body: Demonstrative pronouns (éste, ése, aquél) replace nouns to avoid repetition. They agree in gender and number with the noun they replace.

examples.es: Este mapa es bueno, pero éste es mejor.
examples.en: This map is good, but this one is better.

examples.es: Esa niña es cubana y ésa es china.
examples.en: That girl is Cuban and that one is Chinese.

9.tip: Add an accent to the pronoun (éste) to distinguish it from the adjective (este).

9.title: Demonstrative Adjectives
9.body: Demonstrative adjectives point out specific nouns and agree in gender and number. Use este/esta for 'this', ese/esa for 'that', and aquel/aquella for 'that over there'.
9.tip: Remember the distance: este (close), ese (mid), aquel (far).

9.title: Infinitivo después de preposiciones
9.body: Las preposiciones 'antes de' y 'después de' siempre van seguidas del infinitivo. En verbos reflexivos, el pronombre se une al infinitivo.

examples.es: Me lavo las manos después de comer.
examples.en: I wash my hands after eating.

9.tip: Piensa: 'antes de + acción' y 'después de + acción' - ambas usan la forma base del verbo.
9.source_label: Use of the Infinitive after Prepositions

9.title: Poner vs. Ponerse
9.body: Poner means 'to put' something somewhere. Ponerse is reflexive and means 'to put on' clothing or 'to become' a state/emotion.

examples.es: Pongo el libro en la mesa.
examples.en: I put the book on the table.

9.tip: Ponerse has 'se' for self-action: putting on yourself or a change in yourself.
9.source_label: The Present tense of poner (to put) and ponerse (to put on/to become)

9.title: Demonstrative Adjectives: Este/Ese/Aquel
9.body: Demonstrative adjectives show distance: este (this, near me), ese (that, near you), aquel (that over there, far). They must match the noun's gender and number.
9.tip: Remember: This and these have T's (este/esta/estos/estas). That and those don't (ese/esa/esos/esas).

9.title: Demonstrative Pronouns: This/That
9.body: Spanish has three distances: este (this), ese (that), and aquel (that over there). They must match the noun's gender and number.
9.tip: Think of distances: este (close), ese (mid), aquel (far).

9.title: Direct Object Pronouns
9.body: Replace nouns that receive the action directly. They come before conjugated verbs or attached to infinitives.
9.tip: Think 'who/what' receives the action: lo/la = it, los/las = them.

9.title: Gustar Construction
9.body: With verbs like gustar, the English subject becomes the Spanish indirect object. The thing liked is the grammatical subject.

examples.en: I like coffee. (Coffee is pleasing to me.)

examples.es: A ella le importa la música.
examples.en: She cares about music. (Music matters to her.)

9.tip: Think 'to me is pleasing' instead of 'I like'.
9.source_label: Gustar y otros verbos: Construction Explanation

9.title: Direcciones descriptivas
9.body: En algunos países hispanos, las direcciones usan puntos de referencia en lugar de números de calle. Se describen rutas desde lugares conocidos.

examples.es: Del Cine Momotombo, 1 cuadra a la izquierda y 2 al lago, Managua
examples.en: From Momotombo Cinema, 1 block left and 2 toward the lake, Managua

9.tip: Piensa en dar direcciones como contar un mini-camino desde un lugar famoso.

9.title: Phonology Key Symbols
9.body: The Phonology Key uses special symbols to mark intonation patterns, not normal spelling. These symbols show how phrases should sound when spoken.

examples.en: Normal falling tone of a statement
Rising tone of a polite question
Sharp rise and fall of a familiar question

9.tip: Think: punctuation marks for your voice - period = statement, question mark = question, double question mark = excited question.

9.title: Esto, Eso, Aquello
9.body: Neuter demonstratives refer to unknown or abstract things, not specific nouns. Use them when pointing to something unidentified.
9.tip: Think 'this/that thing' when the object isn't named.

9.title: Solo vs Solamente
9.body: Solo is an adjective meaning 'alone' and must match gender/number. Solamente is an adverb meaning 'only' and can be shortened to sólo.
9.tip: Remember: solo = alone (adjective), solamente/sólo = only (adverb)
9.source_label: NOTE: The adjective solo means alone. It agrees in gender and number with the subject of the sentence. The adverb solamente means only. It can be shortened to sólo (with an accent mark).

9.title: Forming -mente Adverbs
9.body: Add -mente to feminine adjective forms. For adjectives ending in -o, change to -a first. Keep original accent marks.
9.tip: Think: feminine adjective + mente = English -ly

9.title: POR vs PARA: Manner vs Purpose
9.body: Use POR for manner or means (how something is done). Use PARA for purpose or intention (why something is done).

examples.en: We study in order to learn (purpose).

9.tip: POR = Process (how), PARA = Purpose (why)
9.source_label: ¡Atención! More about the Prepositions POR and PARA

9.title: Quisiera - Polite Requests
9.body: Quisiera is the imperfect subjunctive of querer, used for polite requests instead of the conditional querría.

examples.en: I would like a coffee, please.
We would like to reserve a table.

9.tip: Think of 'quisiera' as the polite cousin of 'quiero' - perfect for restaurants and shops.
9.source_label: Quisiera, the imperfect subjunctive form of the verb querer

9.title: Por vs Para: Key Uses
9.body: Por often indicates movement through/via, duration, reason, or exchange. Para typically shows destination, deadlines, purpose, or employment.

examples.es: Necesito el dinero para hoy a las tres.
examples.en: I need the money by three today.

9.tip: POR = Passing through, reason, duration. PARA = Purpose, destination, deadlines.
9.source_label: ADDITIONAL INFORMATION: here are other uses of por and para.

9.title: Demonstrative Adjectives
9.body: Point out people/objects and show distance from speaker. Must match noun's gender and number.
9.tip: Este (here), ese (there), aquel (far) - like pointing with your finger.

9.title: Volver a + infinitivo
9.body: Usamos 'volver a' + infinitivo para expresar repetir una acción. Significa 'hacer algo otra vez' o 'volver a hacer'.
9.tip: Piensa en 'volver' como 'return' y añade 'a' + verbo = 'return to do' la acción.

9.title: Regresar vs Volver vs Devolver
9.body: Regresar and volver both mean 'to return' from a place. Devolver means 'to return' an object. Volver + a + infinitive means 'to do something again'.
9.tip: Devolver = give back (objects). Volver a + verb = do again.
9.source_label: The verbs regresar, volver and devolver (to return)

9.title: Common Spanish Adverbs
9.body: Not all Spanish adverbs end in -mente. Many common ones have unique forms and are used frequently in daily conversation.
9.tip: Remember: mucho (much), poco (little), también (also), tampoco (neither), cerca (near), lejos (far) are all adverbs without -mente.
9.source_label: NOTE: As you already know because of siempre and nunca, not all Spanish adverbs end in -mente. Here are some others that are introduced in this lesson:

9.title: Este vs. Éste
9.body: Este/ese/aquel are adjectives that modify nouns. Éste/ése/aquél are pronouns that replace nouns and have an accent mark.

examples.es: Este mapa es bueno, pero éste es mejor.
examples.en: This map is good, but this one is better.

examples.es: Esa niña es cubana y ésa es china.
examples.en: That girl is Cuban and that one is Chinese.

9.tip: Accent mark = pronoun (stands alone). No accent = adjective (needs a noun).

9.title: Todo/toda/todos/todas + noun
9.body: These adjectives agree in gender/number with the noun and mean 'all', 'every', or 'whole'. No 'de' needed for 'all of'.
9.tip: Match the ending: todo día, toda tarde, todos días, todas tardes.
9.source_label: ¡Atención! Todo, toda, todos, and todas as Adjectives

9.title: También vs Tampoco
9.body: También means 'also/too' in affirmative sentences. Tampoco means 'neither/not...either' in negative sentences. Both can go before or after the verb.

examples.en: I study Spanish. She does too.
I don't eat meat. He doesn't either.

9.tip: También = affirmative, Tampoco = negative. Think: 'También' has 'bien' (good/yes) in it, 'Tampoco' has 'no' in the meaning.

9.title: Creer vs Pensar
9.body: Creer means 'to believe' or 'think that' (opinion). Pensar means 'to think about' (consider) or 'to plan to' (intention).
9.tip: Creer = belief/opinion (often with 'que'). Pensar = mental activity or intention (often with 'en' or infinitive).
9.source_label: The verbs creer and pensar (to think)

9.title: Todo/toda/todos/todas as Pronouns
9.body: When used as pronouns, they replace nouns and must match gender/number. 'Todo/toda' means 'everything' or 'all of it', while 'todos/todas' means 'everyone' or 'all of them'.
9.tip: Remove the noun after 'todo' - if it makes sense, you're using it as a pronoun!
9.source_label: Todo, toda, todos, and todas as Pronouns

9.title: Demonstrative Pronouns vs. Adjectives
9.body: Demonstrative pronouns replace nouns and usually have accent marks to distinguish them from adjectives. They agree in gender and number with the noun they replace.

examples.en: I prefer this book. → I prefer this one.

examples.es: Esas casas son bonitas. (adjective) → Ésas son bonitas. (pronoun)
examples.en: Those houses are pretty. → Those ones are pretty.

9.tip: Accent marks on pronouns (éste, ésta, éstos, éstas) help you remember they're standing alone, not describing a noun.

10.title: Todo/a/os/as as Pronouns
10.body: When used alone without a noun, todo/toda/todos/todas become pronouns meaning 'everything' (singular) or 'everyone/all' (plural).
10.tip: Remove the noun after todo - if it still makes sense, it's a pronoun!
10.source_label: Todo, toda, todos, and todos as Pronouns

10.title: POR vs PARA: Means vs Purpose
10.body: POR often indicates means or method (how something is done), while PARA indicates purpose or intended use (why something is done).
10.source_label: More about the Prepositions POR and PARA

10.title: También vs Tampoco
10.body: También means 'also/too' in affirmative sentences. Tampoco means 'neither/not...either' in negative sentences. Both can go before or after the verb.

examples.en: I study Spanish. She does too.
I don't eat meat. Me neither.

10.tip: También = affirmative too; Tampoco = negative neither. Both share flexible placement.
10.source_label: Culmulative Grammar Notes: Adverbs también and tampoco

10.title: Por vs Para: Key Uses
10.body: Por often indicates movement through, duration, reason, or exchange. Para typically shows destination, deadlines, or working for someone as an employer.

examples.es: Necesito el dinero para hoy a las tres.
examples.en: I need the money by three today.

10.tip: Remember: POR = Passing through, Over time, Reason. PARA = Purpose, Aim, Recipient, Arrival.
10.source_label: ADDITIONAL INFORMATION: here are other uses of por and para.

10.title: Quisiera for Polite Requests
10.body: Use 'quisiera' (imperfect subjunctive of 'querer') for polite 'I would like...' requests. More formal than 'quiero' and more common than 'querría'.

examples.es: Quisiera una mesa para dos, por favor.
examples.en: I would like a table for two, please.
We would like information about the tour.

10.tip: Think 'I would wish...' – it softens requests like 'could I possibly...' in English.

10.title: Regresar vs. Volver vs. Devolver
10.body: Regresar and volver both mean 'to return' from a place. Volver + a + infinitive means 'to do something again.' Devolver means 'to return an object.'
10.tip: Devolver has 'de' like 'give back.' Volver has 'vol-' like 'revolve' or 'repeat.'
10.source_label: The verbs regresar, volver and devolver (to return)

10.title: Creer vs Pensar
10.body: Creer means 'to believe' or 'think that' (opinion). Pensar means 'to think about' (consider) or 'to plan to' (intention).
10.tip: Creer = belief/opinion (often with 'que'). Pensar = mental activity or intention (often with 'en' or infinitive).
10.source_label: The verbs creer and pensar (to think)

10.title: Todo/a/os/as as Adjectives
10.body: Todo/toda/todos/todas agree in gender and number with the noun they describe. Use them before articles (el, la, los, las) to mean 'all' or 'the whole'.
10.tip: Think: 'todo' must match the noun like other adjectives - todo el día (masc), toda la tarde (fem)
10.source_label: Todo, toda, todos, and todas as Adjectives

10.title: Soler + Infinitive
10.body: Expresses habitual actions. Present tense means 'usually do', imperfect means 'used to do'. Only used in these two tenses.

examples.en: I usually run in the park.
I used to study in the afternoons.

10.tip: Think of 'soler' as 'usually' in present, 'used to' in imperfect.

10.title: Direct Object Pronouns
10.body: Direct object pronouns replace nouns that receive the action directly. They come before conjugated verbs.

examples.es: Ella lava los platos → Ella los lava.
examples.en: She washes the dishes → She washes them.
Did they wash the clothes? → Did they wash them?

10.tip: Think 'me, te, lo/la, nos, os, los/las' - these replace what's directly acted upon.

10.title: Acabar de + infinitivo
10.body: Usa 'acabar de' + infinitivo para acciones recién terminadas. Presente: acabo de comer (I just ate). Imperfecto: acababa de comer (I had just eaten).

examples.en: They had just left when you called.

10.tip: Piensa en 'acabar de' como 'just' en inglés para acciones inmediatas.

11.title: Personal a
11.body: Use 'a' before specific people or personified animals as direct objects. It has no English equivalent.
11.tip: Think: 'a' for animate, specific beings. Not for things or general people.

11.title: Indirect Objects with 'a'
11.body: In Spanish, use 'a' before an indirect object to show 'to' or 'for' someone.

examples.es: Le doy el libro a María.
examples.en: I give the book to María.

examples.es: Ella cocina la cena a sus padres.
examples.en: She cooks dinner for her parents.

11.tip: Think of 'a' as the arrow pointing 'to' the person receiving the action.

11.title: Direct Object Pronoun Placement
11.body: Direct object pronouns go before conjugated verbs or attached to infinitives/gerunds. With gerunds, add an accent when attaching.

examples.en: You are going to buy it.

11.tip: Think: 'Before single verbs, after verb pairs' (like infinitives after 'ir a').
11.source_label: The position of direct object pronouns in relation to the verbs

11.title: Indirect Object Pronouns
11.body: Replace 'to/for' + person. They indicate who receives the action indirectly. Place before conjugated verbs or attach to infinitives.

examples.en: I give the book to him/her/you.
I'm going to write a letter to them.

11.tip: Think 'to whom?' or 'for whom?' the action is done.

11.title: Indirect Object Pronouns
11.body: Indirect objects receive the verb's action indirectly, answering 'to/for whom?' Use pronouns like 'le' (to him/her/you formal) and 'les' (to them/you all).

examples.es: Él le sirve café a su esposa.
examples.en: He serves coffee to his wife.

examples.es: Yo les escribo una carta a mis padres.
examples.en: I write a letter to my parents.

11.tip: Remember: 'le' and 'les' replace 'a + person' and often appear before the verb.

11.title: Prepositional Pronouns
11.body: Pronouns after prepositions mostly match subject pronouns, except mí (me) and ti (you familiar). With con, they become conmigo and contigo.
11.tip: Think 'with me' = conmigo, 'with you' = contigo — special forms just for con.

11.title: Indirect Object Pronoun Placement
11.body: Indirect object pronouns go before conjugated verbs. With infinitive constructions, they can go before the conjugated verb or attach to the infinitive.

examples.en: He buys me an ice cream.

examples.es: Ella te va a hacer un sándwich.
examples.en: She is going to make you a sandwich.

examples.es: Ella va a hacerte un sándwich.
examples.en: She is going to make you a sandwich.

11.tip: Think: 'Before or attached' with infinitives, always 'before' with single verbs.
11.source_label: The position of indirect object pronouns in relation to the verbs

11.title: Direct Object Pronouns
11.body: Direct object pronouns replace nouns that receive the verb's action directly. They answer 'what?' or 'whom?' and match the noun's gender and number.

examples.es: Ella toma café → Ella lo toma
examples.en: She drinks coffee → She drinks it

examples.es: Él invita a sus amigas → Él las invita
examples.en: He invites his friends → He invites them

11.tip: Think 'LO for him/it (masc.), LA for her/it (fem.)' - they replace the object directly.

11.title: Por vs Para: Time
11.body: Use 'por' for durations of time (how long something lasts). Use 'para' for deadlines (when something must be done by).

examples.es: Necesito el informe para el viernes.
examples.en: I need the report by Friday.

11.tip: POR = Period Of time, PARA = Point in time (deadline).

11.title: Cara vs. Cada
11.body: Spanish 'd' between vowels sounds soft like English 'th' in 'this', while 'r' is always trilled. Confusing them changes word meanings.
11.tip: Remember: 'd' is soft between vowels (like 'th'), 'r' is always strong - don't let English pronunciation interfere!

11.title: Hace + tiempo + que + pretérito
11.body: This structure expresses how long ago something happened. It can be rearranged with 'hace' at the end.

examples.en: How long ago did you finish?

11.tip: Think 'It makes [time] that...' to remember the structure.
11.source_label: 4. EXPRESIONES DE TIEMPO CON HACER - 3. Hace + expresión de tiempo + que + verb in the preterite tense

11.title: Polite Commands (Ud./Uds.)
11.body: Formal commands use the present subjunctive: 'hable' (speak), 'coma' (eat). For negative, add 'no' before: 'no hable'.
11.tip: Think: opposite vowel endings from present tense 'usted' form (habla → hable).

11.title: Hace + tiempo + que + presente
11.body: Expresa una acción que empezó en el pasado y continúa ahora. Equivale a 'have been' en inglés.

examples.es: Hace tres meses que estamos en Monterey.
examples.en: We have been in Monterey for three months.
How long have you been studying Spanish?

11.tip: Recuerda: 'Hace' marca el tiempo transcurrido, 'que' conecta, y el verbo en presente muestra continuidad.
11.source_label: 4. EXPRESIONES DE TIEMPO CON HACER - 1. Hacer + expresión de tiempo + que + verb in the present tense

11.title: Hacía + tiempo + que + imperfecto
11.body: Expresa una acción que comenzó en el pasado y continuaba hasta otro momento también pasado. Equivale al 'had been doing' en inglés.

examples.es: Hacía tres horas que ella esperaba.
examples.en: She had been waiting for three hours.
How long had you been living here?

11.tip: Hacía marca el tiempo transcurrido, que + imperfecto la acción en progreso.
11.source_label: 4. EXPRESIONES DE TIEMPO CON HACER - 2. Hacía + expresión de tiempo + que + verb in the imperfect tense

11.title: Hace vs. Hacía
11.body: Use 'hace' for actions that started in the past and continue to the present. Use 'hacía' for actions that occurred before another past event.

examples.es: Hace dos años que vivo aquí.
examples.en: I have lived here for two years.

examples.es: Hacía dos años que vivía allí cuando me mudé.
examples.en: I had been living there for two years when I moved.

11.tip: Hace = present perfect, Hacía = past perfect (pluperfect).

11.title: Tener Expressions
11.body: Use tener + noun for feelings where English uses 'to be' + adjective. For emphasis, add mucho/mucha before the noun.
11.tip: Think 'I have hunger' instead of 'I am hungry' to remember the structure.

11.title: Dar + Indirect Object
11.body: Dar is often used with indirect object pronouns to show who receives something. The pronoun comes before the conjugated verb.

examples.es: Yo le doy un regalo a María.
examples.en: I give a gift to María.

11.tip: Think 'to/for whom?' - the answer is your indirect object (me, te, le, nos, os, les).
11.source_label: The Present Tense of dar (to give)

11.title: Gustar Pattern Verbs
11.body: Verbs like gustar use indirect object pronouns (me, te, le, etc.) to show who is affected. The subject is what follows the verb.

examples.es: A mí me gusta el pan.

examples.en: He is interested in the recipe.

11.tip: Think 'it pleases me' instead of 'I like it' to remember the structure.

11.title: Indirect Object Pronouns
11.body: Use 'me' before the verb to mean 'to me'. It can go before the conjugated verb or attached to the infinitive.

examples.en: Can you bring me the check?
Can you bring me the check?

11.tip: Think of 'me' as 'to me' - you're asking someone to do something for you.

11.title: Pronombres Preposicionales
11.body: Pronouns used after prepositions. Same as subject pronouns except mí (me) and ti (you familiar).
11.tip: Con + mí/ti = conmigo/contigo (with me/you)

11.title: Gustar vs. Caer Bien
11.body: Use 'gustar' for attraction or qualities. Use 'caer bien/mal' for general liking/disliking of someone's personality.
11.tip: Think: 'gustar' = romantic/qualities, 'caer bien' = friendly vibe.

11.title: Direct Object Pronouns
11.body: Direct object pronouns replace nouns that receive the verb's action directly. They answer 'what?' or 'whom?' and are placed before conjugated verbs.

examples.es: Ella toma café → Ella lo toma
examples.en: She drinks coffee → She drinks it

examples.es: Él invita a sus amigas → Él las invita
examples.en: He invites his friends → He invites them

11.tip: Pronouns match the object's gender/number: lo/la (sing.), los/las (plur.).

11.title: Direct Object Pronoun Placement
11.body: Direct object pronouns usually go before conjugated verbs. With infinitives or gerunds, they can attach to the verb or stay before the conjugated helper verb.

examples.en: You are going to buy them.

11.tip: Think: 'Little words (pronouns) like to stay close to big verbs, either right before or attached.'
11.source_label: ¡Atención! The position of direct object pronouns in relation to the verbs

11.title: Gustar Structure
11.body: Gustar means 'to be pleasing to.' The subject is what's liked, and gustar agrees with it (gusta for singular, gustan for plural). Use indirect object pronouns (me, te, le, etc.) to show who is pleased.

examples.en: I like the book. (The book is pleasing to me.)
He/She likes movies. (Movies are pleasing to him/her.)

11.tip: Think 'it pleases me' instead of 'I like it' to remember the structure.

11.title: Sobremesa y Siesta
11.body: Two cultural dining customs: 'sobremesa' is lingering conversation after a meal, and 'siesta' is an afternoon rest, especially common in smaller towns.

examples.es: Disfrutamos de una larga sobremesa después de la cena.
examples.en: We enjoyed a long after-dinner conversation after supper.

examples.es: En el pueblo, muchas tiendas cierran para la siesta.
examples.en: In the town, many shops close for the afternoon nap.

11.tip: Remember: 'Sobremesa' = over the table (where you talk); 'Siesta' = sixth hour (traditionally after noon).

12.title: Sufijos -ero/a y -ería
12.body: -ero/a indica profesiones, -ería indica tiendas especializadas. -ería lleva acento en la í.
12.tip: Persona con -ero, tienda con -ería (acento en la í).

12.title: Gustar vs. Caer Bien
12.body: Use 'gustar' for attraction or specific qualities. Use 'caer bien/mal' for general liking/disliking of someone's personality.
12.tip: Gustar = heart attraction, Caer bien = personality click.

12.title: Traer vs Llevar
12.body: Traer means 'to bring' (toward speaker), llevar means 'to take' (away from speaker). Traer is irregular: yo traigo.

examples.en: Can you bring the book here?

examples.es: Voy a llevar esto a la oficina.
examples.en: I'm going to take this to the office.

12.tip: Traer = toward me, Llevar = leave from me
12.source_label: The Present Tense of traer (to bring)

12.title: A + Pronoun/Noun with Gustar
12.body: Use 'a' + pronoun or noun before 'gustar' for clarification or emphasis, in addition to the indirect object pronoun.

examples.es: A mí me gusta el pan.
A los alumnos les gusta la pizza.

12.tip: Think 'To me, it pleases me' – the 'a' marks who it's pleasing TO.

12.title: Double Object Pronouns Order
12.body: When using both indirect and direct object pronouns together, the indirect always comes first. Le/les change to se before lo/la/los/las.
12.tip: Remember 'ID' order: Indirect before Direct, and le/les become se to avoid 'le lo' tongue-twister.
12.source_label: Double object pronouns: indirect and direct object pronouns used together

12.title: Double Object Pronouns: Negative
12.body: In negative sentences with a conjugated verb + infinitive, place 'no' before the pronouns or before the conjugated verb if pronouns attach to the infinitive.

examples.es: Él no nos la va a preparar.
examples.en: He is not going to prepare it for us.
He is not going to prepare it for us.

12.tip: When attaching pronouns to an infinitive, add an accent to maintain stress: preparar → preparárnosla.
12.source_label: Position of double object pronouns in relation to the verbs (Negative constructions)

12.title: Otro/Otra/Otros/Otras
12.body: These adjectives mean 'another' (singular) or 'other' (plural) and must match the noun's gender and number. Add definite articles for 'the other'.
12.tip: Otro = another (masc), otra = another (fem); add 's' for plural: otros/otras = other.
12.source_label: Adjectives otro, otra, otros, and otras

12.title: Gustar Structure
12.body: Gustar means 'to be pleasing to.' The subject is what's liked, and the verb agrees with it. Use indirect object pronouns (me, te, le, etc.) to show who likes it.

examples.en: I like the book. (The book is pleasing to me.)
He/She likes movies. (Movies are pleasing to him/her.)

12.tip: Think 'it pleases me' instead of 'I like it' to remember the structure.

12.title: Posición de pronombres dobles
12.body: Los pronombres dobles siguen las mismas reglas de posición que otros pronombres: antes del verbo conjugado, o unidos al infinitivo/gerundio.

examples.es: ¿Me la va a dar? o ¿Va a dármela?
examples.en: Are you going to give it to me?

12.tip: Recuerda: 'se' reemplaza 'le/les' antes de 'lo/la/los/las'.
12.source_label: Position of double object pronouns in relation to the verbs

12.title: ¿Qué vs Que?
12.body: Accented 'qué' is a question word meaning 'what'. Unaccented 'que' is a relative pronoun meaning 'that' or 'which'.
12.tip: Remember: Question words get the accent mark!

12.title: Syllable Division Rule 1
12.body: Divide Spanish words into syllables after each vowel that forms a syllable, since Spanish prefers syllables ending in vowels.
12.tip: Think 'vowel ends the beat' - each vowel sound marks where to cut.

12.title: Ignoring -s and -n for Accents
12.body: When deciding if a word needs an accent, ignore final -s and -n. Treat the word as if it ends with the preceding letter.
12.tip: Think: 'Sneaky S and N don't count' for accent placement.

12.title: Mucho as Adverb
12.body: Use 'mucho' as an adverb to modify verbs, meaning 'a lot' or 'much'. It doesn't change form.
12.tip: Think: 'mucho' stays the same when describing actions (verbs).

12.title: Otros/Otras
12.body: Use 'otros' (masculine) and 'otras' (feminine) as adjectives before nouns or as pronouns meaning 'others'.
12.tip: Match gender: 'otros' with masculine nouns, 'otras' with feminine nouns.

12.title: Mucho/a/os/as Agreement
12.body: These adjectives must match the noun's gender and number. Use singular with uncountable nouns, plural with countable nouns.
12.tip: Singular = mass nouns (coffee, milk). Plural = countable items (vegetables, vitamins).

12.title: Sufijos -ero/a y -ería
12.body: El sufijo -ero/a indica profesiones, mientras que -ería (con tilde en la i) indica tiendas especializadas donde se vende ese producto o servicio.
12.tip: Recuerda: -ero = persona, -ería = lugar (¡con tilde en la i!)

12.title: Double Object Pronouns
12.body: When using both indirect and direct object pronouns together, the indirect pronoun comes first (me, te, le, nos, os, les) followed by the direct pronoun (lo, la, los, las).
12.tip: Remember 'ID' order: Indirect before Direct pronouns.

12.title: Poco (Adverb)
12.body: Poco means 'little' or 'not much' when used as an adverb to modify verbs, describing a small quantity or degree of an action.
12.tip: Think: poco = 'not a lot' when describing how much you do something.

12.title: Poco: Gender & Number Agreement
12.body: Poco changes form to match the noun's gender and number. Use poco/poca with singular nouns and pocos/pocas with plural nouns.
12.tip: Singular = poco/poca (little), Plural = pocos/pocas (few). Match the ending to your noun.

12.title: Pocos/Pocas as Pronouns
12.body: When used as pronouns, pocos/pocas replace nouns and must match their gender and number. The replaced noun is understood from context.
12.tip: Think: 'pocos' = 'few guys', 'pocas' = 'few gals' - they stand alone when the noun is obvious.

12.title: el otro / la otra
12.body: With definite articles, 'el otro/la otra/los otros/las otras' means 'the other one(s)' in English. It can function as both an adjective and a pronoun.

examples.en: Can you give me the other juice?

12.tip: Match gender/number: otro (masc. sing.), otra (fem. sing.), otros (masc. pl.), otras (fem. pl.).
12.source_label: Grammar Note: el otro/la otra/los otros/las otras

12.title: Muchos/Muchas as Pronouns
12.body: When used as pronouns, muchos/muchas replace a noun and must match its gender and number. The replaced noun is understood from context.

examples.es: Muchos alumnos comen aquí. → Muchos comen aquí.
examples.en: Many students eat here. → Many eat here.

examples.es: Muchas alumnas cenan solas. → Muchas cenan solas.
examples.en: Many students dine alone. → Many dine alone.

12.tip: Think: 'muchos/muchas' stand alone when the noun is obvious from context.

12.title: Regatear vs. Precios Fijos
12.body: En mercados al aire libre es común regatear (negociar precios), pero en almacenes grandes los precios son fijos y no se regatea.

examples.es: Vamos a regatear en el mercado por esta artesanía.
examples.en: Let's bargain at the market for this handicraft.

examples.es: En este almacén los precios son fijos, no se puede regatear.
examples.en: In this department store prices are fixed, you can't bargain.

12.tip: Remember: regatear = outdoor markets, precios fijos = big stores

12.title: Otro/Otra - Adjectives & Pronouns
12.body: Otro/a/os/as must match the noun's gender and number. As adjectives, they mean 'another' (singular) or 'other' (plural). As pronouns, they replace nouns while keeping the same agreement.

examples.en: Not that one, give me another.

12.tip: Think: 'otro' = 'other' but must match like any adjective (o/a/os/as).

12.title: Algo vs Nada
12.body: Algo means 'something' and follows the verb. Nada means 'nothing' and follows 'no' + verb in negative sentences.

examples.en: I cook something in the kitchen.

examples.es: No como nada en la cena.
examples.en: I don't eat anything for dinner.

12.tip: Think: 'algo' = affirmative something, 'nada' = negative nothing (needs 'no')

13.title: Adverbio Mucho
13.body: The adverb 'mucho' means 'a lot' or 'much' and modifies verbs to show intensity or frequency.
13.tip: Think 'mucho' = 'much' + 'o' (like 'a lot-o')

13.title: Adverb 'poco'
13.body: Poco means 'little' or 'not much' in English. It describes the small quantity or degree of an action.
13.tip: Think 'poco' = 'poor quantity' to remember it means 'little'.

13.title: Mucho/a/os/as as Adjectives
13.body: These adjectives agree in gender/number with the noun. Use singular forms with uncountable nouns, plural forms with countable nouns.
13.tip: Singular = uncountable (mucho café), plural = countable (muchos libros).
13.source_label: Adjectives mucho, mucha, muchos, and muchas

13.title: Algo vs Nada
13.body: Use 'algo' for something/anything in affirmative sentences, and 'nada' with 'no' for nothing/not anything in negative sentences.

examples.es: El soldado cocina algo en su cuarto.
examples.en: The soldier cooks something in his room.

examples.es: La mayor no come nada en la cena.
examples.en: The major does not eat anything for dinner.

13.tip: Think: 'algo' = affirmative, 'nada' = negative (needs 'no').

13.title: Poco/a/os/as Agreement
13.body: Poco changes to match the noun's gender and number. Use singular poco/poca with uncountable nouns, plural pocos/pocas with countable nouns.
13.tip: Poco = little (singular), pocos = few (plural). Match the ending to your noun.
13.source_label: Adjectives poco, poca, pocos, and pocas

13.title: Pocos/Pocas as Pronouns
13.body: When used as pronouns, pocos/pocas replace a noun and must match its gender and number. The replaced noun is understood from context.
13.tip: Think: 'pocos/pocas' alone = 'few' as a pronoun; with a noun = 'few' as an adjective.

13.title: Otro as Pronoun
13.body: When otro, otra, otros, otras are pronouns, they replace nouns and must match gender/number. The context tells us what they replace.

examples.en: Can you give me another one?

13.tip: Think: 'otro' = 'another one', 'otros' = 'some others'. Add 'el/la' for 'the other one(s)'.
13.source_label: Pronouns otro, otra, otros, and otras

13.title: Muchos/Muchas as Pronouns
13.body: When used as pronouns, 'muchos' and 'muchas' replace nouns and must match their gender and number. The replaced noun is understood from context.

examples.es: Muchos alumnos comen aquí. → Muchos comen aquí.
examples.en: Many students eat here. → Many eat here.

examples.es: Muchas alumnas cenan solas. → Muchas cenan solas.
examples.en: Many students dine alone. → Many dine alone.

13.tip: Think: 'muchos/muchas' stand alone when the noun is obvious, like saying 'many' instead of 'many people'.

13.title: Futuro de probabilidad
13.body: El futuro simple puede expresar probabilidad o conjetura sobre el presente, equivalente a 'probablemente' + presente.

examples.en: Where could Juan be? (I wonder where he is)
He's probably about thirty years old.

13.tip: Cuando no estás seguro, usa el futuro para adivinar el presente.

13.title: Futuro Simple
13.body: Form the future tense by adding endings directly to the infinitive, without auxiliary verbs like 'will'.

examples.en: I will speak with her tomorrow.

13.tip: The endings are the same for all verbs: -é, -ás, -á, -emos, -éis, -án.

13.title: Parecer - Expressing Opinions
13.body: Use 'parecer' to express opinions or how things appear. '¿Qué le parece?' asks for someone's opinion, while 'Parece que...' states how something seems.

examples.es: ¿Qué le parece si vamos al cine?
examples.en: What do you think if we go to the movies?
It seems like it's going to rain.

13.tip: Think of 'parecer' as 'to appear' - it shows how things appear to you or others.

13.title: Futuro Irregular: Haber
13.body: Haber has an irregular future stem: habr-. The impersonal form 'habrá' means 'there will be'.

examples.en: There will be a party tomorrow.

13.tip: Think: 'haber' → 'habr-' + future endings. Just like 'tener' becomes 'tendr-'.

13.title: Por vs Para: Key Uses
13.body: Por often indicates cause, duration, or substitution. Para typically shows purpose, deadlines, or employment relationships.

examples.en: I am here for six months (duration).

13.tip: POR = Passing Over Reasons (cause/duration). PARA = Purpose And Recipient Aim.
13.source_label: ADDITIONAL INFORMATION: here are other uses of por and para.

13.title: El sistema métrico
13.body: En países hispanohablantes se usa el sistema métrico para medir distancias y estatura, no el sistema imperial.

examples.es: El señor mide un metro setenta.
La distancia es de cinco kilómetros.
examples.en: The distance is five kilometers (about 3.1 miles).

13.tip: Recuerda: 1 km ≈ 0.6 millas, 1 metro ≈ 3.3 pies

13.title: Quedar for Location
13.body: Use quedar like estar to indicate where places are located. It conjugates only in third person (queda/quedan).

examples.es: Los muelles quedan a dos cuadras.
examples.en: The piers are two blocks away.

13.tip: Remember: use 'a' before distances (queda a...), just like with estar.

13.title: POR vs PARA: Movement
13.body: Use POR for movement through or along a route. Use PARA for movement toward a final destination.
13.tip: POR = Path (both start with P), PARA = Final destination
13.source_label: ¡Atención! More about the Prepositions POR and PARA

13.title: Formal Commands
13.body: For formal commands (usted/ustedes), take the 'yo' present form, drop -o, and add -e/-en for -ar verbs or -a/-an for -er/-ir verbs.
13.tip: AR → E/EN, ER/IR → A/AN. Think: 'AR' commands use opposite vowel 'E' from infinitive 'A'.

13.title: Pronouns with Commands
13.body: In affirmative commands, pronouns attach to the verb and require an accent to maintain stress. In negative commands, pronouns go between 'no' and the verb with no accent.
