13.tip: Affirmative: attach + accent. Negative: separate + no accent.

13.title: Expresiones de tiempo
13.body: Para 'to be on time' usa 'llegar a tiempo'. Para 'to be late' hay varias opciones como 'llegar tarde' o 'estar atrasado'. Para preguntar 'how long does it take?' usa '¿Cuánto tarda/demora?'.

examples.es: Llegamos a tiempo para la reunión.
examples.en: We arrived on time for the meeting.
How long does the train take?

13.tip: Recuerda: 'tiempo' para 'on time', 'tarde' para 'late', y 'cuánto' para preguntar duración.

13.title: Commands → Subjunctive
13.body: Formal commands (Ud./Uds.) and nosotros commands share the same forms as the present subjunctive. Learn commands first to master subjunctive later.

examples.en: I want you to go to the counter.

13.tip: Commands are often the subjunctive in disguise—same spelling!

13.title: Recoger Pattern Verbs
13.body: Verbs ending in -ger/-gir change g to j in yo form to keep soft g sound before e.
13.tip: Think: 'ger/gir' verbs get 'j' in yo form - just like recoger → recojo.
13.source_label: ADDITIONAL INFORMATION: verbs with same spelling change as recoger

13.title: Formal Commands Rules
13.body: Formal commands (usted/ustedes) follow present tense patterns: stem changes, yo-form irregularities, and spelling changes for -car/-gar/-zar verbs carry over.
13.tip: Think: 'What happens in present tense, stays in command form' for stem changes and irregularities.

14.title: Idiomatic Expressions with 'ver'
14.body: The verb 'ver' (to see) is used in many common Spanish phrases that don't translate literally.
14.tip: Think of 'ver' as 'checking' or 'confirming' in these expressions.

14.title: Yo-go verbs: coger/escoger
14.body: Verbs ending in -ger/-gir change g to j in yo form to keep soft g sound.
14.tip: Think 'go → jo' for yo form: coger → cojo, escoger → escojo.
14.source_label: Culmulative Grammar Notes: Additional Verbs with First Person Singular Spelling Change

14.title: POR vs PARA: Time
14.body: Use POR for duration (how long something lasts). Use PARA for deadlines (by when something must happen).

examples.es: Necesito el informe para el viernes.
examples.en: I need the report by Friday.

14.tip: POR = Period Of time, PARA = Point in time (deadline).
14.source_label: More about the Prepositions POR and PARA

14.title: -gir/-guar Spelling Changes
14.body: Verbs ending in -gir change g→j before a/o, and -guar verbs add a diaeresis (ü) to preserve the hard g sound.
14.tip: Think: 'gir' becomes 'jir' in subjunctive, 'guar' needs the dots to keep the 'g' sound.
14.source_label: Culmulative Grammar Notes: Additional Spelling Changes (-gir, -guar)

14.title: Quedar for Location
14.body: Use 'quedar' like 'estar' to say where places are located. It's conjugated in third person (queda/quedan).

examples.es: Los muelles quedan a dos cuadras.
examples.en: The piers are two blocks away.

14.tip: Remember: 'quedar' for places, 'estar' for things/people. Both use 'a' for distances.

14.title: Irregular Formal Commands: ser, estar, saber
14.body: Three common verbs have irregular formal commands: ser (sea/sean), estar (esté/estén), and saber (sepa/sepan). These forms must be memorized as they don't follow regular patterns.
14.tip: Remember 'SEA' for ser, 'ESTÉ' for estar, and 'SEPA' for saber - all end with 'a' in singular form.
14.source_label: Culmulative Grammar Notes: Additional Irregular Formal Commands (ser, estar, saber)

14.title: Formal Commands
14.body: Use formal commands with 'usted' or 'ustedes'. Start with the 'yo' present form, drop -o, then add -e(n) for -ar verbs and -a(n) for -er/-ir verbs.
14.tip: Think: Opposite endings - -ar verbs get -e, -er/-ir get -a.

14.title: El Condicional
14.body: The conditional tense uses the infinitive stem plus endings -ía, -ías, -ía, -íamos, -íais, -ían. It's used for hypothetical situations or polite requests.
14.tip: Same endings for ALL verbs - just add to infinitive!

14.title: Condicional: Futuro del Pasado
14.body: El condicional expresa una acción futura desde la perspectiva del pasado. Equivale a 'would + verbo' en inglés.

examples.es: Dijo que vendría a las ocho.
examples.en: He said he would come at eight.
I thought it would rain today.

14.tip: Si el verbo principal está en pasado, el verbo subordinado suele ir en condicional.

14.title: Affirmative Tú Commands
14.body: For affirmative informal commands, use the third person singular (él/ella/Ud.) form of the present tense.
14.tip: Just drop the -s from the tú present form: hablas → habla

14.title: Por vs Para: Additional Uses
14.body: Use POR for substitution, cause, or measurement. Use PARA for employment or specific recipients.

examples.en: I work in place of my sick colleague.
I work for a Spanish company.

14.tip: POR = Passing Over Responsibility, PARA = Pointing At Recipient/Agent
14.source_label: ADDITIONAL INFORMATION: here are other uses of por and para.

14.title: Ver Expressions
14.body: The verb 'ver' (to see) forms common Spanish phrases that don't translate literally. These expressions are used frequently in conversation.
14.tip: Think of 'ver' expressions as fixed phrases - memorize them as complete units rather than translating word-by-word.

14.title: Affirmative Tú Commands
14.body: Use the él/ella/usted present tense form for regular verbs. Drop the -s from the tú form.
14.tip: Think: 'He speaks' → 'You, speak!'

14.title: Affirmative Tú Commands
14.body: Use the él/ella/usted present tense form for regular verbs. Just drop the -s from the tú form.
14.tip: Think: What would he/she do? That's what you tell your friend to do.

14.title: Tú Commands: Placement & Emphasis
14.body: Add 'tú' after the command for emphasis or clarity. Always use 'por favor' to be polite, and use exclamation points in writing to strengthen the order.
14.tip: Think: command + tú (optional) + por favor (essential) = polite emphasis.

14.title: Irregular Affirmative Tú Commands
14.body: Some verbs have irregular forms for affirmative tú commands. They come from the usted command form, dropping the -ga ending.
14.tip: Remember 'Ven Di Sal Haz Ten Ve Pon Sé' for common irregular tú commands.

14.title: Affirmative Tú Commands (Stem-Changers)
14.body: For stem-changing verbs, use the same stem change as in the present tense 'tú' form, but drop the '-s' ending.
14.tip: Just say the 'tú' form without the '-s' (piensas → piensa).

14.title: Ordinal Numbers
14.body: Ordinal numbers (first, second, etc.) agree in gender and number with the nouns they describe. They can be used with or without nouns.
14.tip: Think 'primero' for masculine singular, 'primera' for feminine singular - they change like adjectives.

14.title: Pronouns with Commands
14.body: With affirmative commands, attach pronouns to the verb and add an accent if the verb has 3+ syllables. With negative commands, place pronouns between 'no' and the verb.
14.tip: Affirmative: attach & accent. Negative: sandwich between 'no' and verb.

14.title: Diferencias en pisos
14.body: En países hispanos, la planta baja es el nivel de la calle, mientras que en EE.UU. ese sería el primer piso.

examples.es: Vivo en el primer piso (arriba de la planta baja).
examples.en: I live on the second floor (above ground level).

examples.es: La recepción está en la planta baja.
examples.en: The reception is on the ground floor.

14.tip: Recuerda: 'planta baja' = planta 'baja' al suelo, como 'bajar'.

14.title: Se + verb constructions
14.body: Use 'se' + 3rd person verb for impersonal 'one/they' statements or passive voice when the doer is unknown.
14.tip: Match verb number to subject: singular for 'se habla', plural for 'se venden'.
14.source_label: Impersonal and passive constructions with se + verb

14.title: Subjunctive from Commands
14.body: The familiar tú subjunctive is formed by taking the usted command form and adding -s.

examples.es: Yo quiero que tú escribas una carta.
examples.en: I want you to write a letter.

14.tip: Think: formal command + -s = tú subjunctive
14.source_label: ADDITIONAL INFORMATION: Subjunctive and Command Forms

14.title: Irregular Affirmative Tú Commands
14.body: For hacer, ir, and oír, use these irregular forms: haz, ve, oye. They don't follow the regular -ar/-er/-ir patterns.
14.tip: Remember: 'Haz' sounds like 'has' (you have), 've' is short like 'go', and 'oye' rhymes with 'oye' (hey!).
14.source_label: Affirmative informal commands (irregular verbs: hacer, ir, oír)

15.title: POR vs PARA: Duration vs Deadline
15.body: Use 'por' for duration of time (how long something lasts). Use 'para' for deadlines (when something is due).

examples.en: We are here for six months (duration).

examples.es: Necesito el dinero para las tres.
examples.en: I need the money by three o'clock (deadline).

15.tip: POR = Period Of Time, PARA = Point in time (deadline)
15.source_label: Additional Uses of POR and PARA

15.title: Affirmative Tú Commands
15.body: For regular verbs, use the él/ella/Ud. present tense form. Stem-changing verbs keep their stem change.
15.tip: Just use the 'he/she' form of the verb to tell a friend what to do.

15.title: Imperfect vs. Preterite
15.body: Use imperfect for habitual past actions or ongoing states. Use preterite for completed actions with specific time frames.

examples.en: I worked there for three years.

15.tip: Imperfect = 'used to' or 'was/were -ing'. Preterite = specific completed events.

15.title: Condicional de probabilidad
15.body: Use the conditional tense to express probability or conjecture about past events, similar to how the future tense expresses probability about the present.

examples.en: I wonder where Enrique was last night.
He probably went to the movies.

15.tip: Think 'would have' for past probability - 'He would have been at the movies' = probability.

16.title: Por vs Para: Key Uses
16.body: Use POR for duration, exchange, or cause. Use PARA for deadlines, purpose, or employment.

examples.en: I am here for six months (duration).
I need the money by today (deadline).

16.tip: POR = Passing Over Reasons (duration/cause). PARA = Purpose And Recipient Aim.
16.source_label: Use POR when you mean / Use PARA when you mean

16.title: Pronoun Placement in Commands
16.body: In affirmative commands, pronouns attach to the verb. In negative commands, pronouns go between 'no' and the verb.
16.tip: Affirmative: stick them on. Negative: keep them separate.
16.source_label: ¡Atención! The position of reflexive pronouns, direct and indirect object pronouns in command forms

16.title: Ordinal Numbers: Gender & Number
16.body: Ordinal numbers must match the noun's gender and number. Primero and tercero shorten to primer/tercer before masculine singular nouns.
16.tip: Think: ordinals dress like their nouns—masculine/feminine, singular/plural.

16.title: Se + verb constructions
16.body: Use 'se' + verb for impersonal statements (unspecified subject) or passive voice (focus on action, not doer).
16.tip: Impersonal: always singular verb. Passive: verb matches subject (singular/plural).
16.source_label: ¡Atención! Impersonal and passive constructions with se + verb

16.title: Conditional Perfect Conjecture
16.body: Used to speculate about what probably happened in the past. Equivalent to 'would have' or 'probably had' in English.

examples.en: Why hadn't they come? (I wonder why)

16.tip: Think: 'would have' for guessing past events you didn't witness.
16.source_label: Repaso De Las Estructuras Gramaticales: Conditional Perfect Conjecture

16.title: Future Perfect Conjecture
16.body: Use the future perfect (haber + past participle) to guess what probably happened in the past, like English 'must have' or 'probably'.

examples.en: Where could she have put the keys?

16.tip: Think 'future perfect' for 'past probability' - it's guessing backward!
16.source_label: Repaso De Las Estructuras Gramaticales: Future Perfect Conjecture

16.title: Pretérito vs Imperfecto
16.body: Use preterite for completed past actions (what happened). Use imperfect for ongoing or habitual past actions (what was happening).

examples.en: I used to study Spanish every day.

16.tip: Pretérito = one-time events. Imperfecto = repeated habits or ongoing states.

16.title: Preterite Spelling Changes: -guar verbs
16.body: Verbs ending in -guar change u to ü in the yo form of the preterite to keep the 'gw' sound.
16.tip: Think: the two dots (diaeresis) make the 'u' sound separate from the 'g'.
16.source_label: Verbs with orthographic changes: Additional Information

16.title: Preterite Tense Basics
16.body: The preterite describes completed past actions. For regular -ar verbs, drop -ar and add -é, -aste, -ó, -amos, -asteis, -aron.

examples.es: El soldado estudió español el año pasado.
examples.en: The soldier studied Spanish last year.

16.tip: Preterite answers 'What happened?' - think of it as a snapshot of a completed past event.

17.title: Hace + tiempo
17.body: Use 'hace' with present tense for ongoing actions (duration), and with preterite for completed actions (how long ago).

examples.es: Hace dos meses que estudio español.
examples.en: I have been studying Spanish for two months.
I arrived here two weeks ago.

17.tip: Present = still doing it. Preterite = already did it.
17.source_label: LECCION 17 ¡Atención! Idiomatic uses of hace with time expressions

17.title: Preterite Tense
17.body: Used for completed past actions, answering 'What happened?' It focuses on the event itself, not its duration.

examples.es: El soldado estudió español el año pasado.
examples.en: The soldier studied Spanish last year.

17.tip: Think of it as a snapshot of a finished past moment.

17.title: Stem-changers in Preterite
17.body: Verbs that change e→ie or o→ue in present tense are regular in preterite. No stem changes occur.

examples.en: Today I think, yesterday I thought.
Today they return, yesterday they returned.

17.tip: Present tense stem-changers take a break in the past!

17.title: -car → -qué
17.body: For -car verbs in the preterite yo form, change c to qu to keep the hard /k/ sound before e.
17.tip: Think: 'car' becomes 'qué' - like asking 'what?' in Spanish.

17.title: -gar verbs in preterite
17.body: For -gar verbs in the yo form of the preterite, change g to gu to keep the hard g sound before e.
17.tip: Think: 'g' needs a 'u' bodyguard before 'e' to stay hard.

17.title: Preterite vs Present: Nosotros
17.body: The nosotros form of regular -ar and -ir verbs is identical in preterite and present tense. Context tells you which tense is being used.
17.tip: Look for time clues like 'ayer' (yesterday) or 'hoy' (today) to identify the tense.

17.title: Zar Verbs in Preterite
17.body: Verbs ending in -zar change z to c in the yo form of the preterite tense to maintain pronunciation.
17.tip: Think: 'Z' becomes 'C' only for 'yo' in the past.

17.title: Preterite Spelling Changes: c→qu & u→ü
17.body: In preterite yo form, verbs ending in -car change c→qu (indiqué), and verbs ending in -guar add a diaeresis: u→ü (averigüé).
17.tip: Think: 'car' becomes 'quar', 'guar' gets two dots to keep the 'g' hard.
17.source_label: Verbs with orthographic changes: Additional Information

17.title: -uir Verbs: Present & Preterite
17.body: In present tense, add 'y' before endings except nosotros/vosotros. In preterite, follow regular -er/-ir patterns with accent on 'y'.
17.tip: Think 'y' for 'you' (except 'we/you all') in present, and keep the 'y' in preterite too.

17.title: Irregular Preterite: Ir/Ser
17.body: Ir (to go) and ser (to be) share identical preterite forms. Context determines which verb is being used.

examples.en: I went to the movies yesterday.

17.tip: Think: 'Fui' can mean 'I went' or 'I was'—the story tells you which.
17.source_label: Grammar Notes: Irregular verbs in the preterite

17.title: Preterite e>i Stem Change
17.body: For -ir verbs like pedir, the stem changes e to i only in third person preterite (él/ella/usted, ellos/ellas/ustedes). Other forms are regular.
17.tip: Think 'third person only' - the stem change happens just for 'he/she/they' in the past.
17.source_label: Grammar Notes: Stem-changing -ir verbs (e>i in preterite)

17.title: Preterite vs Imperfect Meaning Changes
17.body: Some verbs change meaning in the preterite tense. The preterite often indicates a single completed action or change, while the imperfect describes ongoing states.

examples.en: I found out the truth yesterday.

17.tip: Think: Preterite = change/event, Imperfect = ongoing state.
17.source_label: Verbs that change meaning when used in the preterite

17.title: Preterite Endings
17.body: For regular verbs, drop the infinitive ending and add preterite endings. -ar verbs use one set, while -er and -ir verbs share identical endings.

examples.es: hablar → hablé, hablaste, habló, hablamos, hablasteis, hablaron
examples.en: to speak → I spoke, you spoke, he spoke, we spoke, you all spoke, they spoke

examples.es: comer → comí, comiste, comió, comimos, comisteis, comieron
examples.en: to eat → I ate, you ate, he ate, we ate, you all ate, they ate

17.tip: AR verbs get ARon endings, ER/IR verbs get IERon endings for 'they' form.

17.title: Querer: Preterite vs Imperfect
17.body: Use preterite for specific refusals or changes of desire. Use imperfect for ongoing desires or general unwillingness.

examples.en: She refused to clean her room (preterite).
He didn't want to get up (imperfect, general unwillingness).

17.tip: Preterite = refused; Imperfect = didn't want to
17.source_label: VERB EXERCISES Querer, Preterite and Imperfect

17.title: Present Participle (Gerund)
17.body: The present participle (gerundio) is a verb form ending in -ando or -iendo, used for ongoing actions.
17.tip: Remember: -AR verbs → -ando, -ER/-IR verbs → -iendo

17.title: Pretérito Perfecto vs Pluscuamperfecto
17.body: Pretérito Perfecto (haber + participio) describes past actions relevant to the present. Pluscuamperfecto (había + participio) describes actions completed before another past action.

examples.es: Hemos tenido que ir a hablar con ella.
examples.en: We have had to go speak with her.

examples.es: Yo había visto muchas películas antes.
examples.en: I had seen many movies before.

17.tip: Perfecto = present relevance. Pluscuamperfecto = 'past before the past'.

17.title: Present Participle (Gerund)
17.body: Used with 'estar' to form progressive tenses, showing ongoing actions. Formed by adding -ando to -ar verbs and -iendo to -er/-ir verbs.
17.tip: Think '-ing' in English: studying → estudiando, running → corriendo.

17.title: Present Participle Uses
17.body: Spanish present participles can show means (by doing) or simultaneous action after verbs, but cannot function as nouns like English gerunds.

examples.en: We learn by repeating the words.

examples.es: Salió diciendo que no se sentía bien.
examples.en: She left saying she didn't feel well.

17.tip: Think 'by doing' or 'while doing' for these uses, but use infinitives for nouns (nadar, not nadando).
17.source_label: OTHER USES OF THE PRESENT PARTICIPLE

17.title: Hace + tiempo
17.body: Use 'hace' with time expressions to show duration (present tense) or how long ago something happened (preterite).

examples.es: Hace dos años que vivo aquí.
examples.en: I have lived here for two years.

17.tip: Present = ongoing action; Preterite = completed action.
17.source_label: ESTRUCTURAS: Idiomatic uses of hace with time expressions

17.title: Conocer vs. Saber
17.body: Conocer means to be familiar with people, places, or things. Saber means to know facts, information, or how to do something.

examples.en: I know that today is Tuesday.

17.tip: Conocer = acquaintance, Saber = facts

17.title: Desde + Time
17.body: Use 'desde' to indicate when an action started. It can be followed by a specific point in time or 'desde hace' for duration.

examples.en: I've been here since three o'clock.

examples.es: Vivo aquí desde hace dos años.
examples.en: I've lived here for two years.

17.tip: Think: 'desde' = starting point, 'desde hace' = duration from past to now.

17.title: Voz Pasiva con Ser
17.body: La voz pasiva se forma con cualquier tiempo del verbo 'ser' + participio pasado. El participio concuerda en género y número con el sujeto.
17.tip: Piensa: 'ser' + acción recibida. Más común en noticias (estilo periodístico).
17.source_label: ¡Atención! More about passive constructions: introduction to the passive voice

18.title: Conocer vs. Saber
18.body: Use 'conocer' for familiarity with people, places, or things. Use 'saber' for facts, information, or skills.
18.tip: Think: conocer = to be acquainted with, saber = to know a fact.
18.source_label: ¡Atención! The Spanish verbs conocer and saber

18.title: Tanto vs Tan
18.body: Tanto means 'so much' and modifies verbs or nouns. Tan is the shortened form before adjectives and means 'so'.
18.tip: Tanto = so much, Tan = so (before adjectives)

18.title: Tanto/tanta vs tantos/tantas
18.body: Use tanto/tanta with uncountable nouns (so much) and tantos/tantas with countable nouns (so many). They must match the noun's gender and number.
18.tip: Tanto = so much (singular), Tantos = so many (plural). Match the ending to your noun.
18.source_label: Adjectives tanto, tanta, tantos, and tantas

18.title: Imperfect Tense Uses
18.body: Describes ongoing past actions, habits, or background conditions. It answers 'what was happening?' or 'what used to happen?'

examples.en: I used to study Spanish every day.
It was four o'clock when she arrived.

18.tip: Think 'was/were -ing' or 'used to' for repeated past actions.

18.title: Voz Pasiva
18.body: La voz pasiva se forma con ser + participio pasado. El sujeto recibe la acción, no la realiza. Es común en noticias.
18.tip: Recuerda: 'ser' + participio (concuerda en género/número con el sujeto).
18.source_label: Culmulative Grammar Notes - Passive Voice Introduction

18.title: Saber vs Enterarse
18.body: Use 'saber' for knowing facts or skills, and 'enterarse' for discovering or finding out new information.

examples.en: He knew how to play the piano.

examples.es: Él se enteró de que ella tocaba el piano.
examples.en: He found out that she used to play the piano.

18.tip: Saber = knowledge you already have; Enterarse = knowledge you just acquired.
18.source_label: TO KNOW HOW vs TO FIND OUT

18.title: Imperfecto: verbos regulares
18.body: El imperfecto describe acciones habituales o en progreso en el pasado. Para verbos regulares, usa terminaciones específicas según la conjugación.

examples.es: Yo hablaba con mi abuela cada domingo.
examples.en: I used to talk with my grandmother every Sunday.

examples.es: Ellos comían en ese restaurante frecuentemente.
examples.en: They used to eat at that restaurant frequently.

18.tip: AR → aba, ER/IR → ía. ¡ER e IR comparten las mismas terminaciones!

18.title: Voz Pasiva
18.body: En la voz pasiva, el sujeto recibe la acción. Se forma con 'ser' + participio pasado, que concuerda en género y número con el sujeto.

examples.es: El puente fue destruido por los terroristas.
examples.en: The bridge was destroyed by the terrorists.

examples.es: La carta fue escrita por mi madre.
examples.en: The letter was written by my mother.

18.title: Imperfecto: Usos
18.body: El imperfecto describe acciones habituales, repetidas o en progreso en el pasado. También indica la hora en el pasado.

examples.en: I used to study Spanish every day.
It was four o'clock when she arrived.

18.tip: Think 'used to' or 'was/were -ing' in English for habitual past actions.

18.title: Números romanos
18.body: Para números romanos, se usan ordinales del 1 al 10 y cardinales del 11 en adelante al leerlos en voz alta.

18.title: Superlativo Absoluto
18.body: Add -ísimo/-ísima to adjectives to mean 'very/extremely'. Drop the final vowel first if present, and match gender/number.

18.title: Tanto vs. Tan
18.body: Use 'tanto/tanta/tantos/tantas' before nouns to mean 'so much/many', matching gender/number. Use 'tan' before adjectives to mean 'so'.
18.tip: Tanto = noun, Tan = adjective. Both mean 'so' in English.

18.title: Lo + Superlative
18.body: Use 'lo' with superlatives to refer to abstract concepts or general ideas, not specific things.

examples.es: Lo más difícil fue hablar con él.
examples.en: The most difficult (thing) was talking to him.

examples.es: Lo mejor es que Ud. le habló.
examples.en: The best (thing) is that you spoke to him.

18.tip: Think of 'lo' as 'the thing that is' before the superlative.
18.source_label: Superlatives and the neuter pronoun lo

18.title: Imperfect Regular Verbs
18.body: Regular verbs in the imperfect follow predictable patterns. For -ar verbs, add -aba endings; for -er/-ir verbs, add -ía endings.

examples.en: I used to talk with my friend.
They used to eat at home.

18.tip: Think: -ar verbs use 'aba' endings, -er/-ir verbs use 'ía' endings. Only ir, ser, and ver are irregular.

18.title: Superlativos: más/menos + adjetivo
18.body: Use 'el/la/los/las + más/menos + adjective + de' to express 'the most/least' in Spanish. Adjectives must match the noun in gender and number.

examples.es: Ella es la estudiante más inteligente de la escuela.
examples.en: She is the most intelligent student in the school.

examples.es: Este es el libro menos interesante de la biblioteca.
examples.en: This is the least interesting book in the library.

18.tip: Remember: 'más' = most, 'menos' = least. The adjective always agrees with the noun it describes.

18.title: Números romanos
18.body: Los números romanos usan letras para valores: I=1, V=5, X=10, L=50, C=100, D=500, M=1000. Una letra más pequeña a la izquierda resta, a la derecha suma.
18.tip: Izquierda resta, derecha suma. Como leer: de izquierda a derecha, si el siguiente valor es mayor, resta el actual.

19.title: Lo + Superlative
19.body: Use 'lo' with superlatives to refer to abstract concepts or qualities, not specific things.

examples.es: Lo más difícil fue hablar con él.
examples.en: The most difficult thing was talking to him.

examples.es: Lo mejor es que Ud. le habló.
examples.en: The best thing is that you spoke to him.

19.tip: Think of 'lo' as 'the thing that is' before the superlative.
19.source_label: Superlatives and the neuter pronoun lo

19.title: Superlativo Absoluto
19.body: Add -ísimo/-ísima to adjectives to mean 'very/extremely'. Drop the final vowel first if present, and match gender/number.
19.tip: Think: adjective + 'ísimo' = 'super' adjective. Drop final vowel before adding.

19.title: Superlativos Irregulares
19.body: Los adjetivos 'bueno' y 'malo' tienen formas superlativas irregulares: 'mejor' y 'peor'. Van precedidos del artículo definido y concuerdan en número con el sustantivo.

examples.es: Este libro es el mejor de todos.
examples.en: This book is the best of all.

19.title: Irregular Comparisons: Bueno/Malo
19.body: For comparisons of inequality, bueno becomes mejor (better) and malo becomes peor (worse). These irregular forms agree in number with the noun and are followed by que.

examples.es: Este café es bueno, pero ese es mejor.
examples.en: This coffee is good, but that one is better.

examples.es: Mi día fue malo, pero el tuyo fue peor.
examples.en: My day was bad, but yours was worse.

19.tip: Think: 'Good gets better, bad gets worse' — both switch to shorter Spanish words.

19.title: Números Romanos
19.body: Para números romanos, usa ordinales (primero, segundo) hasta 10, y cardinales (once, doce) desde 11 en adelante.
19.tip: Recuerda: 10 y menos = ordinales, 11 y más = cardinales.

19.title: Comparisons: Equality
19.body: Use 'tan...como' with adjectives/adverbs and 'tanto...como' with verbs to express equality.

examples.es: Ella corre tan rápido como él.
examples.en: She runs as fast as him.
I study as much as you.

19.title: Preterite vs Imperfect
19.body: Use preterite for completed actions and specific events. Use imperfect for ongoing actions, descriptions, and habitual past actions.

examples.es: Bolívar vivía en España cuando conoció a su esposa.
examples.en: Bolívar was living in Spain when he met his wife.

19.title: Preterite vs Imperfect
19.body: Use preterite for completed actions and imperfect for ongoing or habitual past actions. The choice depends on how you view the event.

examples.es: Cuando era niño, leía todos los días.
examples.en: When I was a child, I read every day.

19.tip: Think: Preterite = snapshot (one-time events), Imperfect = video (ongoing/habitual actions).

19.title: Comparisons of Equality
19.body: Use 'tanto/a' with mass nouns and 'tantos/as' with count nouns, both followed by 'como' to express 'as much/many...as'.

examples.en: I have as much patience as you.
There are as many books as magazines.

19.tip: Match 'tanto' endings to the noun: -o for masculine, -a for feminine, add -s for plural.

19.title: Comparisons of Equality
19.body: Use 'tan...como' with adjectives/adverbs, and 'tanto/a(s)...como' with nouns. 'Tanto' agrees in gender/number with the noun it modifies.

examples.es: Juan es tan simpático como Rosa.
examples.en: Juan is as nice as Rosa.

examples.es: Juan bebe tanto café como Rosa.
examples.en: Juan drinks as much coffee as Rosa.

examples.es: Juan tiene tantos amigos como Rosa.
examples.en: Juan has as many friends as Rosa.

19.tip: Tan for traits (adjectives), tanto for quantities (nouns).

19.title: Se + Person Subject
19.body: Use 'se' with third person singular verb when the subject is a person and the doer is indefinite or unknown.

examples.en: The guilty one will be punished.

19.tip: Think 'someone will punish' → focus stays on the person receiving action.
19.source_label: Repaso De Las Estructuras Gramaticales - Se construction with person as subject (Section 5)

19.title: Voz Pasiva con Se
19.body: Usa 'se' + verbo en tercera persona para expresar acciones donde no se menciona quién las hace. El verbo concuerda con el sujeto en número.
19.tip: Piensa en 'se' como 'uno' o 'la gente' para entender la acción impersonal.
19.source_label: Repaso De Las Estructuras Gramaticales - Pseudo Passive Voice (Section 4)

19.title: Construcción Impersonal con 'Se'
19.body: Usa 'se' + verbo en tercera persona singular para expresar acciones generales sin especificar quién las realiza.

examples.en: It is said that it will rain tomorrow.

examples.es: Se cree en la buena suerte.

19.tip: Piensa en 'se' como 'one' en inglés o 'la gente' en español.
19.source_label: Repaso De Las Estructuras Gramaticales - Impersonal or agentless construction (Section 6)

19.title: Verbs with Preterite Meaning Shifts
19.body: Some verbs change meaning in the preterite tense, often indicating a single completed action rather than an ongoing state.
19.tip: Think: Preterite = punctual event, Imperfect = ongoing state.
19.source_label: Verbs that change meaning when used in the preterite

19.title: Estuvo vs Estaba + -ndo
19.body: Use 'estuvo + -ndo' for completed past actions with a clear end. Use 'estaba + -ndo' for ongoing past actions, often interrupted or with no defined end.

examples.en: It was raining all morning (and stopped).
It was raining when I left (ongoing).

19.tip: Estuvo = done deal. Estaba = in progress, often with 'cuando'.
19.source_label: The preterite progressive and the imperfect progressive: expressing actions in progress in the past

19.title: Pretérito vs Imperfecto
19.body: Use pretérito for completed past actions (nació, luchó). Use imperfecto for ongoing states, habits, or descriptions in the past (estaba, vivía, viajaba).

examples.es: Bolívar vivía en España cuando la conoció.
examples.en: Bolívar was living in Spain when he met her.

19.tip: Pretérito = snapshot (one-time events). Imperfecto = video (ongoing background).

20.title: Past Participle Functions
20.body: Past participles have four main uses: with ser for passive voice, with estar for resulting states, as adjectives, and with haber for perfect tenses.
20.tip: Remember: SER for action, ESTAR for state, HABER for perfect tenses, and they can stand alone as adjectives.

20.title: Estuvo vs Estaba + -ndo
20.body: Use 'estuvo + -ndo' for completed past actions with a clear endpoint. Use 'estaba + -ndo' for ongoing past actions without a defined end, often interrupted.

examples.en: It was raining all morning (and stopped).
It was raining when I left (ongoing).

20.tip: Estuvo = done deal. Estaba = in progress, often with 'cuando'.
20.source_label: The preterite progressive and the imperfect progressive

20.title: Alguno vs. Ninguno
20.body: Use 'alguno/algunos' for 'some/any' in affirmative contexts and 'ninguno/ninguna' for 'none/not any' in negative contexts. Both drop the final -o before masculine singular nouns.
20.source_label: Adjectives alguno, alguna, algunos, algunas, ninguno, ninguna - Example

20.title: Algún vs. Ningún
20.body: Use 'algún' in affirmative questions and 'ningún' in negative statements to mean 'any' or 'a'.

examples.en: No, I didn't buy any book.

20.source_label: Shortened forms algún and ningún - Example

20.title: Alguno vs. Ninguno
20.body: Use 'alguno/algunos' for 'some/any' in affirmative sentences and 'ninguno/ninguna' for 'none/not any' in negative sentences.

examples.en: Yes, there are some important ones.

20.source_label: Pronouns alguno, alguna, algunos, algunas, ninguno, ninguna - Example

20.title: Buen/Mal vs Bueno/Malo
20.body: Use 'buen' and 'mal' before masculine singular nouns, but 'bueno' and 'malo' after nouns or alone.
20.tip: Think: 'buen' and 'mal' are the short forms for 'bueno' and 'malo' before masculine nouns.
20.source_label: Shortened forms: buen and mal - Example

20.title: Resultant State with Estar
20.body: Use estar + past participle to describe a current condition that resulted from a completed action, not the action itself.

examples.en: The party is organized (in an organized state now).
The door is closed (in a closed state now).

20.tip: Think: 'How is it now?' not 'What happened?'
20.source_label: Grammar Explanation: Resultant State with Estar

20.title: Estar + participio pasado
20.body: Usamos 'estar' + participio pasado para describir el estado resultante de una acción completada, como un objeto que ha sido transformado.
20.tip: Piensa en 'estar' + participio como 'is/has been + past participle' en inglés para estados resultantes.
20.source_label: H. Escriba oraciones completas en español que expresen el estado en que se encuentran las cosas o personas como resultado de la acción que se indica entre paréntesis. Siga el modelo:

20.title: Se for Accidents
20.body: Use 'se' + indirect object + verb to express unplanned events that happen to someone unintentionally, like accidents or mishaps.

examples.en: We forgot the appointment (it slipped our minds).

20.tip: Think 'it happened TO me' – the indirect object (me/te/le/nos/les) shows who it happened to.
20.source_label: Grammar Note: Se for Unplanned Events

20.title: Accidental 'Se'
20.body: Use 'se' + indirect object pronoun to express unplanned or accidental events, shifting focus from who did it to what happened.

examples.es: A Yolanda siempre se le pierden las llaves.

20.tip: Think 'it happened to me' not 'I did it' - se me olvidó = it slipped my mind.
20.source_label: Grammar Examples: Unplanned Occurrences with Se

21.title: Present Perfect
21.body: Formed with 'haber' + past participle. Used for past actions that connect to the present.

examples.en: I have talked with my uncle.

21.title: Haber as Auxiliary Verb
21.body: Haber is used as a helping verb to form perfect tenses, paired with an invariable past participle ending in -o.
21.tip: Think 'haber + -o' for perfect tenses, unlike 'tener' for possession.

21.title: Past Perfect (Pluperfect)
21.body: Formed with imperfect of 'haber' + past participle. Used for actions completed before another past action.

examples.es: Él había oído las noticias antes de llegar.
examples.en: He had heard the news before arriving.

examples.es: Ya habían terminado cuando yo llegué.
examples.en: They had already finished when I arrived.

21.tip: Think 'had + past participle' in English - same time relationship!

21.title: Ya vs Todavía
21.body: Ya means already, todavía means still. Add 'no' to negate: ya no (no longer), todavía no (not yet).
21.tip: Ya = done; Todavía = ongoing. Add 'no' to flip the meaning.

21.title: Alguien vs. Nadie
21.body: Alguien means 'someone' or 'anyone' in affirmative contexts. Nadie means 'no one' or 'nobody' in negative contexts. Both refer only to people.

examples.en: Did anyone come to the party?

21.tip: Think: Alguien = affirmative, Nadie = negative. Both start with 'n' in English: aNyoNe vs. No one.
21.source_label: More about affirmative and negative expressions

21.title: Subjuntivo en cláusulas nominales
21.body: Usa el subjuntivo después de 'que' cuando el verbo principal expresa voluntad, emoción o duda, y hay sujetos diferentes en ambas cláusulas.

examples.en: I want you to study more.

examples.es: Dudo que él sepa la respuesta.
examples.en: I doubt he knows the answer.

21.tip: Recuerda WED: Will, Emotion, Doubt → Subjuntivo.
21.source_label: 1. LOS USOS DEL SUBJUNTIVO (UNA VISTA GLOBAL)

21.title: Adjetivos Apocopados
21.body: Some Spanish adjectives shorten before masculine singular nouns. Primero → primer, bueno → buen, grande → gran.
21.tip: Remember: 'buen', 'mal', 'gran' come before the noun, not after.

21.title: Alguien vs. Nadie
21.body: Alguien means 'someone' and nadie means 'no one'. Both refer only to people. In statements, they usually come before the verb.
21.tip: Think: alguien = 'a person', nadie = 'not a person'.

22.title: Acabar de + infinitivo
22.body: Use 'acabar de + infinitive' to say something just happened. Equivalent to English 'have just + past participle'.

examples.es: Acabo de hablar con el doctor.
examples.en: I have just spoken with the doctor.

examples.es: Juan acababa de volver cuando llamaste.
examples.en: Juan had just returned when you called.

22.title: Forming Present Subjunctive
22.body: Use formal commands for most forms. For tú, use negative familiar commands. For nosotros, use -emos for -ar verbs and -amos for -er/-ir verbs.

examples.es: Quiero que Ud. cante una canción.
examples.en: I want you to sing a song.

examples.es: Le pido que lea esta historia.
examples.en: I ask you to read this story.

22.tip: Irregularities in present tense or commands carry over to subjunctive.

22.title: Se for Unplanned Events
22.body: Use 'se + verb' to describe accidental or unplanned events, shifting focus from who did it to what happened.
22.tip: Think 'Oops!'—when something happens unintentionally, Spanish often uses 'se' to show it wasn't on purpose.
22.source_label: More about constructions with se + verb: unplanned events

22.title: Past Participles: Accents & Irregulars
22.body: For -er/-ir verbs with stems ending in a, e, or o, add an accent to -ído (e.g., traer → traído). Some verbs have completely irregular forms.
22.tip: Remember 'traído, leído, oído' need accents because the stem vowel 'steals' the stress.

22.title: Other Perfect Tenses
22.body: Beyond present and past perfect, Spanish uses future perfect (habrán + participle) for actions completed by a future time, and conditional perfect (habría + participle) for hypothetical past actions.

examples.es: Ya habrán solucionado el problema para mañana.
examples.en: They will have solved the problem by tomorrow.
I would have solved it quickly.

22.tip: Think 'will have' = future perfect, 'would have' = conditional perfect.

22.title: Ya vs Todavía
22.body: Ya indicates completion ('already'), while todavía indicates continuation ('still'). With 'no', ya no means 'no longer' and todavía no means 'not yet'.
22.tip: Think: ya = done, todavía = ongoing. Add 'no' to flip the meaning.

22.title: Past Participle Functions
22.body: Past participles have four main uses: with 'ser' for passive voice, with 'estar' for resulting states, as adjectives, and with 'haber' for perfect tenses.

examples.es: Las casas fueron destruidas por el terremoto.
examples.en: The houses were destroyed by the earthquake.
The buildings are destroyed (as a result).

22.tip: Remember: 'ser' + participle = action (passive), 'estar' + participle = resulting state, 'haber' + participle = perfect tense.

23.title: Cuando + Subjunctive
23.body: Use the present subjunctive with 'cuando' when the main clause refers to a future possibility. No 'que' is needed.

examples.es: Las cosas cambiarán cuando él sea presidente.
examples.en: Things will change when he becomes president.

examples.es: Ella quiere ayudar cuando tenga dinero.
examples.en: She wants to help when she has money.

23.tip: Future uncertainty → subjunctive. Present/past facts → indicative.
23.source_label: Culmulative Grammar Notes: More uses of the present subjunctive: with cuando

23.title: POR vs PARA: For Whom
23.body: Use POR for substitution (in place of someone) and PARA for employment (working for someone).

examples.es: María trabaja hoy por su padre.
examples.en: María works today in place of her father.
María works for her father (as her boss).

23.source_label: ¡Atención! More about the Prepositions POR and PARA

23.title: Tal vez, quizá(s), ojalá, puede que
23.body: Use present subjunctive after these expressions to show uncertainty or hope. No 'que' needed with tal vez/quizá(s).

examples.es: Tal vez María esté en casa.

23.tip: Think: 'Maybe I hope it's possible' → all trigger subjunctive for uncertainty.
23.source_label: More uses of the present subjunctive: with tal vez, quizá(s), ojalá, and puede que

23.title: Past Subjunctive Tenses
23.body: Use the imperfect subjunctive for past wishes or doubts, and the past perfect subjunctive for actions completed before another past action.

examples.es: Yo quería que él viniera a la fiesta.
examples.en: I wanted him to come to the party.

examples.es: No creía que lo hubieran invitado.
examples.en: I didn't believe they had invited him.

23.tip: Remember: 'viniera' = past wish, 'hubieran invitado' = action before past belief.
23.source_label: Additional Information: Other Subjunctive Tenses Examples

23.title: Tener ganas de
23.body: Use 'tener ganas de + infinitive' to express wanting or feeling like doing something. For others doing something, use 'tener ganas de que + subjunctive'.

examples.en: We feel like having you come.

23.tip: Think: 'I have desires of...' → 'tener ganas de'

23.title: Por vs Para
23.body: Use 'por' for movement through, duration, or reason. Use 'para' for destination, purpose, or deadlines.
23.tip: POR = Passing through, PARA = Pointing toward.

23.title: POR vs PARA: Recipient vs Agent
23.body: Use PARA for the recipient of an action, and POR for the agent in passive voice constructions.

examples.es: Este regalo es para mi madre.
examples.en: This gift is for my mother.

examples.es: La carta fue escrita por Juan.
examples.en: The letter was written by Juan.

23.tip: PARA = for someone (recipient), POR = by someone (agent in passive)
23.source_label: More about the Prepositions POR and PARA

23.title: Artículos con países
23.body: Algunos países usan artículo definido opcionalmente (el/la Argentina). El Salvador y La Habana siempre lo llevan porque es parte del nombre.

examples.es: Vivo en el Perú / Vivo en Perú

23.tip: Recuerda: 'El' Salvador siempre lleva su 'el' como parte del nombre oficial.
23.source_label: ¡Atención! Using Spanish definite articles in geographical names

23.title: Verbs like gustar
23.body: These verbs use indirect object pronouns (me, te, le, nos, les) to show who is affected, followed by the thing that causes the feeling or action.
23.tip: Think: 'To me is pleasing...' not 'I like...'
23.source_label: ESTRUCTURAS: Verbs that follow the grammatical pattern of gustar

24.title: Verbs Like Gustar
24.body: These verbs use indirect object pronouns (me, te, le, etc.) to show who is affected, followed by the verb agreeing with the subject.
24.tip: Think 'to be pleasing to' instead of 'to like'—the structure mirrors English phrases like 'It matters to me.'
24.source_label: ADDITIONAL INFORMATION: These verbs also follow the grammatical pattern of gustar.

24.title: Verbs like gustar
24.body: These verbs use indirect object pronouns (me, te, le, nos, les) and agree with the subject that follows them, not the person experiencing the action.
24.tip: Think: 'Things please/interest/lack ME' not 'I like/lack things'.
24.source_label: ¡Atención! Verbs that follow the grammatical pattern of gustar

24.title: Sentirse (to feel)
24.body: Sentirse is a reflexive verb used to express emotions or physical states. It always requires a reflexive pronoun that matches the subject.
24.tip: Think 'myself feel' - the reflexive pronoun shows who is feeling.

24.title: Aunque: Indicative vs Subjunctive
24.body: Use indicative when aunque means 'although' (established fact). Use subjunctive when aunque means 'even if' (uncertainty).

examples.es: Sale aunque hay toque de queda.
examples.en: He goes out although there is a curfew.

examples.es: Saldrá aunque haya toque de queda.
examples.en: He will go out even if there is a curfew.

24.source_label: The Indicative or Subjunctive with Adverbial Conjunctions

24.title: Subjuntivo en cláusulas adverbiales
24.body: Usa el subjuntivo presente con ciertas conjunciones adverbiales cuando la acción principal expresa futuro o incertidumbre. Algunas conjunciones siempre requieren subjuntivo.

examples.en: I will study until you arrive.

examples.es: Lo haré a menos que me lo prohíbas.
examples.en: I'll do it unless you forbid me.

24.tip: Recuerda 'PARA CASI': Para que, A menos que, Con tal que, Antes que, Sin que, A no ser que → siempre subjuntivo.
24.source_label: Culmulative Grammar Notes: More uses of the present subjunctive: with adverbial clauses

24.title: Diminutivos y Aumentativos
24.body: Sufijos como -ito/-ita (diminutivo) y -ote/-ota (aumentativo) modifican sustantivos y adjetivos para indicar tamaño, afecto o desprecio.
24.tip: Quita la vocal final antes de añadir el sufijo: 'mesa' → 'mesita'.

24.title: Omitir 'que' con antes/después/hasta
24.body: Cuando hay un solo sujeto, se omite 'que' en antes de, después de, hasta. El segundo verbo va en infinitivo, no subjuntivo.

24.title: Faltar Idioms
24.body: Faltar often appears in common expressions that don't translate literally. These idioms describe needs, obligations, or unusual situations.
24.tip: Think of faltar as 'to lack' - these idioms express what's missing or needed.
24.source_label: ADDITIONAL INFORMATION: In Spanish, faltar is frequently used in idiomatic expressions.

24.title: Subjuntivo en Cláusulas Adjetivales
24.body: Usa el subjuntivo cuando el antecedente (persona/cosa/lugar) es inexistente o indefinido. Usa el indicativo cuando el antecedente es definido o existe.

examples.es: No hay nadie que hable quechua.
examples.en: There isn't anyone who speaks Quechua.

examples.es: Busco un intérprete que sepa español.
examples.en: I'm looking for an interpreter who knows Spanish.

24.tip: ¿Incierto o negativo? → Subjuntivo. ¿Seguro y existente? → Indicativo.

24.title: Gustar-like Verbs
24.body: These verbs work like 'gustar' - the subject is what causes the feeling, and the person affected uses indirect object pronouns.
24.tip: Think 'it pleases me' not 'I like it' - the thing does the action to the person.

24.title: Se + Verb: Unplanned Events
24.body: Use 'se' + verb to describe accidental or unplanned events, often avoiding blame. Add indirect object pronouns (me, te, le, etc.) to show who it happened to.

examples.es: A mí se me acabaron las tabletas.

24.tip: Think 'Oops!' – this construction often describes accidents you didn't mean to cause.
24.source_label: ¡Atención! More about constructions with se + verb: unplanned events

24.title: Se + verb for accidents
24.body: Use 'se' + third person verb to describe unplanned events or accidents, shifting focus from who did it to what happened.

examples.en: The glass broke on me (I accidentally broke the glass).
The keys were forgotten by him (He forgot the keys).

24.tip: Think 'it happened to me' not 'I did it' - the object becomes the subject.

24.title: Acabar de + infinitivo
24.body: Use 'acabar de + infinitive' to say something just happened. It's like English 'have just + past participle'.

examples.es: Acabo de hablar con el doctor.
examples.en: I have just spoken with the doctor.

examples.es: Juan acababa de volver cuando llamaste.
examples.en: Juan had just returned when you called.

24.title: Re- Prefix Verbs
24.body: Many Spanish verbs starting with 're-' indicate repetition or intensity of the base action. This prefix often corresponds to English 're-' or adds emphasis.
24.tip: Think 're-' = again or back, like English 'redo' or 'return'.

25.title: Imperfect Subjunctive with Impersonal Expressions
25.body: Use the imperfect subjunctive after impersonal expressions in the past, like 'era importante que', to express necessity, importance, or emotion about uncertain past actions.

examples.en: It was important that you study history.
It was necessary that we arrive early.

25.tip: If the main clause is in the past (era/fue), the subordinate clause often needs imperfect subjunctive.
25.source_label: More uses of the imperfect subjunctive: with impersonal expressions

25.title: Doubt/Denial + Imperfect Subjunctive
25.body: Use the imperfect subjunctive after expressions of doubt or denial in the past, like 'dudaba que' or 'negó que'.

examples.es: Él dudaba que la situación mejorara pronto.
examples.en: He doubted that the situation would improve soon.

examples.es: Ella negó que trabajara con ellos.
examples.en: She denied that she worked with them.

25.tip: Past doubt/denial → imperfect subjunctive. Think: 'doubted/denied' triggers 'ara/iera' endings.
25.source_label: More uses of the imperfect subjunctive: with expressions of doubt and denial

25.title: Imperfect Subjunctive with Volition
25.body: Use the imperfect subjunctive after verbs expressing desire, preference, or request in the past. It shows the action was desired but not necessarily completed.

examples.es: Quería que tú vinieras a la fiesta.
examples.en: I wanted you to come to the party.
She preferred that we study more.

25.tip: Think: Past wish → imperfect subjunctive. If the main verb is in preterite or imperfect, the subordinate verb needs imperfect subjunctive.
25.source_label: Uses of the imperfect subjunctive: with verbs of volition

25.title: Advanced Subjunctive Tenses
25.body: Beyond present and imperfect subjunctive, Spanish uses compound tenses like present perfect and past perfect subjunctive for completed actions in dependent clauses.

examples.es: Espero que ya lo hayan invitado.
examples.en: I hope they have already invited him.

examples.es: No creía que lo hubieran invitado.
examples.en: I didn't believe they had invited him.

25.tip: Think 'have/had' + past participle in English = perfect subjunctive in Spanish.
25.source_label: ADDITIONAL INFORMATION: Other subjunctive tenses

25.title: Imperfect Subjunctive with Emotion Verbs
25.body: Use the imperfect subjunctive after past-tense emotion verbs (like temer, sentir) when there are two different subjects, connected by 'que'.

examples.es: Temían que los rebeldes atacaran otra vez.
examples.en: They feared that the rebels would attack again.

examples.es: Me alegraba de que ella estuviera aquí.
examples.en: I was glad that she was here.

25.tip: Past emotion + que → imperfect subjunctive for the second action.
25.source_label: ¡Atención! More uses of the imperfect subjunctive: with verbs that express emotion

25.title: Cuando + Imperfect Subjunctive
25.body: Use the imperfect subjunctive with 'cuando' when the main clause verb is in the past and refers to a future possibility from that past perspective.

examples.es: Ella dijo que quería ayudar cuando tuviera dinero.
examples.en: She said she wanted to help when she had money.

examples.es: Prometí que llamaría cuando llegara a casa.
examples.en: I promised I would call when I arrived home.

25.tip: Think: Past main verb + cuando = imperfect subjunctive for future-in-the-past events.
25.source_label: ¡Atención! More uses of the imperfect subjunctive: with cuando

25.title: Quisiera - Polite Requests
25.body: Use quisiera (imperfect subjunctive of querer) for polite requests instead of querría (conditional). It's more formal and courteous.

examples.en: I would like to ask you for a favor.

examples.es: Quisiera reservar una mesa para dos.
examples.en: I would like to reserve a table for two.

25.tip: Think: 'I would like' = quisiera (polite), not querría (less common).
25.source_label: NOTE: The imperfect subjunctive form of querer, quisiera

25.title: Alternate -se Endings
25.body: The imperfect subjunctive has two sets of endings: -ra and -se forms. Both are correct and interchangeable, used after dropping -ron from the preterite ellos form.

examples.en: If I had more time, I would travel.
It was important that they came.

25.tip: Think of -se as the 'secondary' set—both work equally well!
25.source_label: ADDITIONAL INFORMATION: Alternate imperfect subjunctive endings

25.title: Hubiera (hay in imperfect subjunctive)
25.body: The impersonal form 'hay' becomes 'hubiera' in the imperfect subjunctive, used for hypothetical or uncertain situations in the past.

examples.en: It was important that there be food.
I doubted there was enough time.

25.tip: Think: 'hay' → 'hubiera' for past uncertainty, like 'there would be' in English.
25.source_label: NOTE: The imperfect subjunctive of hay

25.title: Imperfect Subjunctive
25.body: Used in dependent clauses when the main verb is in the past (preterite, imperfect, or past perfect). Also used for hypothetical situations with the conditional.

examples.es: Sugerí que él viniera a clase.
examples.en: I suggested that he come to class.
I wanted you to read it to me.

25.tip: Form it by dropping -ron from the ellos preterite form and adding -ra, -ras, -ra, -ramos, -ran.
25.source_label: ¡Atención! The imperfect subjunctive

25.title: Imperfect Subjunctive in Adverbial Clauses
25.body: Use the imperfect subjunctive in adverbial clauses when the main verb is in the past. It follows the same rules as the present subjunctive, but shifted to past time.

examples.es: Los rebeldes atacaron antes de que los soldados llegaran.
examples.en: The rebels attacked before the soldiers arrived.

examples.es: Dijo que iba a salir aunque hubiera toque de queda.
examples.en: He said he was going to go out even if there were a curfew.

25.tip: Past main verb? Switch present subjunctive to imperfect subjunctive in the clause.
25.source_label: ¡Atención! More uses of the imperfect subjunctive: with adverbial clauses

25.title: Neuter 'lo' as abstract noun
25.body: Use 'lo' + adjective or past participle to create abstract nouns meaning 'the [adjective] thing' or 'what [verb]'.

examples.en: The interesting thing is the story.

25.tip: Think of 'lo' as turning descriptions into concepts: 'lo bueno' = 'the good (thing)'
25.source_label: 1. USES OF THE NEUTER "LO”

25.title: Pero vs Sino vs Sino que
25.body: Use 'pero' for contrast, 'sino' to correct a negative statement with an alternative, and 'sino que' before a conjugated verb.

examples.es: No solo no ayudó, sino que molestó.
examples.en: He not only didn't help, but he bothered us.

25.tip: Sino follows 'no' when replacing a false idea with a true one.
25.source_label: Traduzca la expresión but usando pero, sino o sino que según cada caso.

25.title: PERO vs SINO vs SINO QUE
25.body: Use 'pero' for general contrast. Use 'sino' after a negative to correct with a noun/infinitive. Use 'sino que' after a negative to correct with a conjugated verb.

examples.en: I don't study, but I work.
It's expensive, but I'll buy it.

25.tip: Sino needs 'no' before it. Sino que needs a full clause after.
25.source_label: PERO, SINO, SINO QUE

25.title: Imperfect Subjunctive with Volition
25.body: Use imperfect subjunctive after past tense verbs of wanting/requesting when there are two different subjects.

examples.en: I wanted you to read the book.
She asked that we arrive early.

25.tip: Past volition + que + imperfect subjunctive = two different people involved
25.source_label: ¡Atención! Uses of the imperfect subjunctive: with verbs of volition

25.title: Como si + imperfect subjunctive
25.body: Always use imperfect subjunctive after 'como si' (as if), regardless of the main verb tense.

examples.es: Ella actúa como si fuera famosa.
examples.en: She acts as if she were famous.

examples.es: Hablan como si lo supieran todo.
examples.en: They speak as if they knew everything.

25.tip: Think: 'como si' triggers the 'ra/se' endings automatically.
25.source_label: ADDITIONAL INFORMATION: Imperfect subjunctive with como si

25.title: Imperfect Subjunctive with Impersonal Expressions
25.body: Use the imperfect subjunctive after impersonal expressions (es importante, es necesario, etc.) when the main verb is in the past tense.

examples.en: It was necessary that you arrive early.

examples.es: Fue una lástima que no pudieran venir.
examples.en: It was a pity that they couldn't come.

25.tip: Past main verb? Use imperfect subjunctive after que.
25.source_label: ¡Atención! More uses of the imperfect subjunctive: with impersonal expressions

25.title: Tal vez/Quizá/Ojalá/Puede que + Imperfect Subjunctive
25.body: Use the imperfect subjunctive after these expressions to express doubt, possibility, or hope about past events or hypothetical situations.

examples.en: I hope you would arrive on time.

25.tip: Present subjunctive = present/future possibility. Imperfect subjunctive = past/hypothetical possibility.
25.source_label: ADDITIONAL INFORMATION: Imperfect subjunctive with tal vez, quizá(s), ojalá, and puede que

25.title: Doubt/Denial + Imperfect Subjunctive
25.body: Use the imperfect subjunctive after expressions of doubt or denial (dudar, negar, no creer) when referring to past situations.

examples.es: Él dudaba que la situación mejorara.
examples.en: He doubted that the situation would improve.

examples.es: Ella negó que trabajara con ellos.
examples.en: She denied that she worked with them.

25.tip: Past doubt? Use imperfect subjunctive after que.
25.source_label: ¡Atención! More uses of the imperfect subjunctive: with expressions of doubt and denial

25.title: Imperfect Subjunctive in Adjectival Clauses
25.body: Use the imperfect subjunctive in adjectival clauses when the main verb is in the past and the antecedent is indefinite or negative.

examples.es: No había nadie que hablara aimara.
examples.en: There wasn't anyone who spoke Aymara.

examples.es: No existía ningún lugar que fuera perfecto.
examples.en: There didn't exist any place that was perfect.

25.tip: Remember: Past main verb + indefinite/negative antecedent = imperfect subjunctive in the adjective clause.
25.source_label: ¡Atención! More uses of the imperfect subjunctive: with adjectival clauses

25.title: Imperfect Subjunctive Pronoun Placement
25.body: Pronouns attach to the end of the imperfect subjunctive verb when the verb is in the affirmative command structure within a subordinate clause.

examples.en: I wanted you to read it to me.

examples.es: Le había dicho que se afeitara.
examples.en: He had told you to shave.

25.tip: Think: 'Attach pronouns to the subjunctive verb when it's giving an indirect command.'
25.source_label: The imperfect subjunctive: Pronoun Placement Example

25.title: Imperfecto del subjuntivo
25.body: Used for hypothetical or uncertain situations in the past, often following certain triggers like emotion, doubt, or volition.

examples.en: I wanted you to come yesterday.
It was possible that it would rain.

25.tip: Think of it as the 'past version' of the present subjunctive.
25.source_label: Lesson 31 Objectives: Structures

26.title: Subjunctive Mood
26.body: Use the subjunctive for uncertain or desired actions, often after 'que'. It expresses possibility, not fact.

examples.en: I suggest that he read this book.
I want you to read it to me.

26.tip: Think WEDDING: Wishes, Emotions, Doubt, Desires, Impersonal expressions, Negation, God/general statements.
26.source_label: The Subjunctive

26.title: Indicative vs. Subjunctive
26.body: Use indicative for events that actually happened or are factual. Use subjunctive for hypothetical, uncertain, or desired situations.

examples.es: Dijo que metieron en la cárcel a los rebeldes.
examples.en: He said they put the rebels in jail.

examples.es: Espero que pongan a los rebeldes en la cárcel.
examples.en: I hope they put the rebels in jail.

26.tip: Indicative = reality, Subjunctive = possibility
26.source_label: Cumulative Grammar Notes: Indicative vs. Subjunctive

26.title: Como si + Imperfect Subjunctive
26.body: Always use imperfect subjunctive after 'como si' (as if), regardless of the main verb tense.

examples.en: He talks as if he were rich.

examples.es: Actúan como si no pasara nada.
examples.en: They act as if nothing were happening.

26.tip: Think: 'como si' always triggers the 'ra' form subjunctive, even in present tense sentences.
26.source_label: Cumulative Grammar Notes: Imperfect Subjunctive with Como si

26.title: Imperfect Subjunctive in Adjectival Clauses
26.body: Use imperfect subjunctive in adjectival clauses when the main verb is past tense and the antecedent is indefinite or doesn't exist.

examples.es: No había ningún libro que explicara eso.
examples.en: There wasn't any book that explained that.

examples.es: No conocía a nadie que supiera la respuesta.
examples.en: I didn't know anyone who knew the answer.

26.tip: Past main verb + indefinite/negative antecedent = imperfect subjunctive
26.source_label: Cumulative Grammar Notes: Imperfect Subjunctive in Adjectival Clauses

26.title: Would for Repeated Past Actions
26.body: Use the imperfect tense (not conditional) when 'would' describes habitual past actions. This matches English usage for repeated behaviors.

examples.es: Cuando era niño, jugaba al fútbol todos los días.
examples.en: When I was a child, I would play soccer every day.

examples.es: Mi abuela siempre nos contaba cuentos.
examples.en: My grandmother would always tell us stories.

26.tip: Think 'used to' - if you can replace 'would' with 'used to' in English, use imperfect in Spanish.
26.source_label: Lesson 32: Conditional Footnote - Would for Repeated Action

26.title: Tal vez, quizás, ojalá, puede que
26.body: Use present subjunctive for present/future uncertainty, imperfect subjunctive for past. No 'que' with tal vez/quizás; optional with ojalá.

examples.en: I hope you would arrive on time.

26.tip: Present = maybe now/future; imperfect = maybe past. Remember: tal vez/quizás stand alone.
26.source_label: Cumulative Grammar Notes: Subjunctive with Tal vez, Quizás, Ojalá, Puede que

26.title: Conditional for Projected Events
26.body: Use the conditional to express a projected event when the main clause is in the past tense.

examples.es: Dijeron que él llegaría a las dos.
examples.en: They said that he would arrive at two.
I thought it would rain today.

26.tip: Past + conditional = reported future (like 'would' in English).
26.source_label: More uses of the conditional: to express a projected event

26.title: Conditional of Poder
26.body: The verb 'poder' (to be able to) is irregular in the conditional tense. It uses the stem 'podr-' for all forms instead of the infinitive.
26.tip: Remember: poder → podr- + conditional endings (-ía, -ías, -ía, -íamos, -íais, -ían)
26.source_label: Irregular verbs in the conditional tense - Examples

26.title: Conditional for Past Curiosity
26.body: Use the conditional tense to express curiosity about something in the past, similar to how the future tense expresses present curiosity.

examples.en: I wonder how old he was when he died.
I wonder where they were yesterday.

26.tip: Think: 'would' for past wonder. Future = present wonder, Conditional = past wonder.
26.source_label: More uses of the conditional: to express curiosity

26.title: Si + Imperfect Subjunctive + Conditional
26.body: Use this structure for hypothetical or unlikely situations. The 'si' clause sets an unreal condition, and the main clause gives the imagined result.

examples.es: Si yo fuera rico, viajaría por el mundo.
examples.en: If I were rich, I would travel the world.

examples.es: Compraría esa casa, si tuviera el dinero.
examples.en: I would buy that house, if I had the money.

26.tip: Think 'If I were... I would...' in English. Both verbs are in the 'past' form in Spanish too.
26.source_label: ¡Atención! More uses of the conditional: to express hypothetical situations

26.title: Llevar + tiempo
26.body: Use 'llevar' with time expressions to indicate how long something has been happening, similar to 'hace...que' construction.

examples.es: Llevo tres años viviendo en Madrid.
examples.en: I have been living in Madrid for three years.
How long have you been studying Spanish?

26.tip: Think: 'I carry with me this time doing something' → duration up to now.
26.source_label: 4. “LLEVAR” EN EXPRESIONES DE TIEMPO

26.title: Comparaciones con 'de lo que'
26.body: Use 'de lo que' when comparing adjectives, adverbs, or whole ideas, not specific nouns. It translates as 'than what...' in English.

examples.es: Esto es más fácil de lo que te imaginas.
examples.en: This is easier than what you imagine.

examples.es: Corre más rápido de lo que pensaba.
examples.en: He runs faster than what I thought.

26.tip: Think 'de lo que' = 'than the thing that' for non-noun comparisons.
26.source_label: 3. ORACIONES COMPARATIVAS

26.title: Hacía vs Llevar
26.body: Both express duration of past actions. 'Hacía' uses 'que' + imperfect, while 'llevar' uses imperfect + gerund/infinitive.

examples.en: I had been living there for years.
I had been living there for years.

26.tip: Think: 'Hacía que' = 'It had been that', 'Llevaba' = 'I was carrying' the time.
26.source_label: Repaso De Las Estructuras Gramaticales

26.title: Subjunctive with Volition Verbs
26.body: Use present subjunctive after verbs expressing wishes, requests, or influence when there are two different subjects. Follow with 'que'.

examples.en: I want you to study more.
She recommends that we arrive early.

26.tip: Different subjects? Use subjunctive. Same subject? Use infinitive: 'Quiero estudiar' (I want to study).
26.source_label: Lesson 26 Grammar Notes: Uses of the Present Subjunctive

26.title: Tener ganas de
26.body: Use 'tener ganas de + infinitive' to express wanting or feeling like doing something. Add 'no' before 'tener' for the negative.
26.tip: Think of 'ganas' as 'the urge' — you have the urge to do something.
26.source_label: ¡Atención! Another expression with tener

26.title: Subjunctive from Yo Irregulars
26.body: Verbs irregular in the present tense 'yo' form keep that same stem change in the present subjunctive for all persons.

examples.es: Espero que yo ponga la mesa.
examples.en: I hope I set the table.
I want you to leave early.

26.tip: Use the 'yo' present stem (like 'pong-' from 'pongo') and add subjunctive endings.
26.source_label: Verbs irregular in the yo form of the present tense in the present subjunctive

26.title: Present Subjunctive Formation
26.body: Use formal commands for most forms, negative tú command for tú, and special endings for nosotros. For nosotros, use -emos for -ar verbs and -amos for -er/-ir verbs.
26.tip: Think: 'formal command' for most, 'no' + tú command for tú, and swap endings for nosotros.
26.source_label: The present subjunctive formation

26.title: Conditional: Would/Could
26.body: Used to express what would or could happen in hypothetical situations or given certain conditions.

examples.es: Yo votaría, pero no soy ciudadano.
examples.en: I would vote, but I'm not a citizen.

26.tip: Think 'would' or 'could' in English → Spanish conditional (-ía endings).
26.source_label: ¡Atención! Uses of the conditional: to express what would or could happen

26.title: Irregular Subjunctive Patterns
26.body: Verbs irregular in present tense or commands keep those same irregularities in present subjunctive. Use their 'yo' form present indicative, drop -o, add subjunctive endings.

examples.es: Quiero que ellos paguen lo que deben.
examples.en: I want them to pay what they owe.

examples.es: Espero que tú conozcas a mi familia.
examples.en: I hope you meet my family.

26.tip: Think: 'Irregular in present? Stay irregular in subjunctive!'
26.source_label: Irregular verbs in the present subjunctive

26.title: Irregular Subjunctive Commands
26.body: Some irregular commands like 'dé' (dar) and 'vaya' (ir) match their present subjunctive forms. Use them after expressions like 'quiero que...' to express wishes or requests.

examples.en: I want you to be here tomorrow.

examples.es: Es posible que haya más libros.
examples.en: It's possible there are more books.

26.tip: Remember DIES: Dar, Ir, Estar, Ser (and Saber) have irregular subjunctive forms that double as formal commands.
26.source_label: Lesson 26 Grammar Notes: Irregular Command Forms & Subjunctive

26.title: Stem-changing subjunctive
26.body: Stem-changing verbs keep their original stem in nosotros/as forms, except e→i verbs which change throughout. Dormir changes to u in nosotros/as.

examples.es: que yo piense / que nosotros pensemos
examples.en: that I think / that we think

examples.es: que yo pida / que nosotros pidamos
examples.en: that I ask for / that we ask for

26.tip: Nosotros keeps original stem, except e→i verbs and dormir (durmamos).
26.source_label: Stem-changing verbs in the present subjunctive

26.title: Deber Conditional = Should
26.body: The conditional form of deber expresses 'should' for advice or mild obligation, unlike present deber which means 'must'.
26.tip: Think: 'deber' present = must (strong), 'deber' conditional = should (softer advice)
26.source_label: NOTE: The verb deber

26.title: Irregular Conditional Verbs
26.body: The same 12 verbs irregular in future tense are irregular in conditional. Use their irregular stems plus conditional endings (-ía, -ías, etc.).

examples.en: They could/would be able to come.

26.tip: Remember: 'decir' becomes 'dir-', 'hacer' becomes 'har-', 'tener' becomes 'tendr-' - same as future tense stems.
26.source_label: Irregular verbs

26.title: Conditional for Past Curiosity
26.body: Use the conditional tense to express curiosity about something in the past, similar to how the future tense expresses present curiosity.

examples.en: I wonder how old he was when he died.
I wonder where my keys were yesterday.

26.tip: Think: 'would' for past wonder. Future = present wonder, Conditional = past wonder.
26.source_label: ¡Atención! More uses of the conditional: to express curiosity

26.title: Would: Past Habit vs. Conditional
26.body: Use imperfect tense for 'would' describing repeated past actions, not conditional tense. Conditional is for hypothetical situations.

examples.es: De niño, siempre jugaba al fútbol.
examples.en: As a child, I would always play soccer.
If I had time, I would travel more.

26.tip: Remember: 'Would' + past action = imperfect; 'Would' + hypothetical = conditional.
26.source_label: Conditional footnote - Imperfect vs. Conditional

26.title: Conditional for Projected Events
26.body: Use the conditional tense when reporting past statements about future events. It's like 'would' in English indirect speech.

examples.es: Dijeron que él llegaría a las dos.
examples.en: They said that he would arrive at two.
I thought it would rain today.

26.tip: Present main clause → future tense. Past main clause → conditional tense.
26.source_label: ¡Atención! More uses of the conditional: to express a projected event

26.title: Monarquía vs. Democracia
26.body: La monarquía concentra el poder en un rey hereditario, mientras la democracia distribuye el poder mediante elecciones populares.

examples.es: España tiene una monarquía constitucional con un rey como jefe de estado.
examples.en: Spain has a constitutional monarchy with a king as head of state.

examples.es: En una democracia, los ciudadanos votan para elegir a sus representantes.
examples.en: In a democracy, citizens vote to elect their representatives.

26.tip: Recuerda: 'mono-' significa uno (un solo gobernante), 'demo-' significa pueblo (gobierno del pueblo).
26.source_label: Definitions of Political and Economic Systems

27.title: Impersonal Expressions + Subjunctive
27.body: Use the subjunctive in subordinate clauses after impersonal expressions like 'es importante que' to show necessity or opinion.

examples.en: It's important that you study Spanish.
It's necessary that we arrive early.

27.tip: Think: impersonal expression + que + subjunctive verb.
27.source_label: Note on Impersonal Expressions with Subordinate Clause Subject Examples

27.title: Present Perfect Subjunctive
27.body: Formed with present subjunctive of 'haber' plus past participle. Used in dependent clauses when main clause requires subjunctive.

examples.en: I hope they have already arrived.

examples.es: Dudo que haya terminado el trabajo.
examples.en: I doubt he has finished the work.

27.tip: Think: 'subjunctive haber' + 'past participle' = uncertainty about completed actions.

27.title: Condicional perfecto
27.body: Expresses what would have happened in the past. Formed with conditional of 'haber' + past participle of main verb.

examples.en: I would have traveled to Spain.
They would have finished the work.

27.tip: Think 'would have' + past action. Similar to English structure.

27.title: Como si + Past Perfect Subjunctive
27.body: Use the past perfect subjunctive after 'como si' (as if) to describe hypothetical past situations, regardless of the main verb's tense.

examples.es: Habla como si hubiera visto un fantasma.
examples.en: He talks as if he had seen a ghost.

examples.es: Actúan como si no hubieran comido en días.
examples.en: They act as if they hadn't eaten in days.

27.tip: Think: 'como si' always triggers the subjunctive mood for unreal past scenarios.

27.title: Past Perfect Subjunctive
27.body: Formed with imperfect subjunctive of 'haber' + past participle. Used in dependent clauses when the main clause requires imperfect subjunctive.

examples.es: No creía que ellos ya hubieran usado las computadoras.
examples.en: I didn't think they had already used the computers.

examples.es: Era improbable que ella hubiera llegado temprano.
examples.en: It was unlikely that she had arrived early.

27.tip: Think 'hubiera/hubiese' + past participle for 'had done' in hypothetical past situations.

27.title: Futuro Perfecto
27.body: Expresses actions that will be completed before a future point. Formed with future tense of 'haber' plus past participle.

examples.es: Para mañana, habré terminado el proyecto.
examples.en: By tomorrow, I will have finished the project.

examples.es: Ellos habrán llegado antes de las ocho.
examples.en: They will have arrived before eight o'clock.

27.tip: Think 'will have + past participle' in English.

27.title: Como si + Imperfect Subjunctive
27.body: Use 'como si' followed by the imperfect subjunctive to express hypothetical comparisons ('as if'). It always triggers the subjunctive mood.

examples.es: Habla como si lo supiera todo.
examples.en: He speaks as if he knew everything.

examples.es: Conducía como si no hubiera tráfico.
examples.en: He drove as if there were no traffic.

27.tip: Think: 'como si' = 'as if' → always requires the 'ra/se' subjunctive endings.
27.source_label: ¡Atención! Using the expression como si . . . (as if . . . )

27.title: Haber + estado + gerundio
27.body: This structure expresses continuous action in the past, similar to English 'has/had been doing'. Use 'haber' + 'estado' + present participle.

examples.es: He estado estudiando español por dos años.
examples.en: I have been studying Spanish for two years.

examples.es: Había estado lloviendo toda la noche.
examples.en: It had been raining all night.

27.tip: Think 'have/had been -ing' → 'haber estado -ando/-iendo'
27.source_label: ¡Atención! Using haber + the past participle of estar + present participle of main verb

27.title: Past Perfect Subjunctive + Conditional Perfect
27.body: Use this structure for hypothetical past situations that didn't happen. The 'if' clause uses past perfect subjunctive, and the result uses conditional perfect.

examples.en: If I had studied, I would have passed.

examples.es: Habríamos viajado si hubiéramos tenido dinero.
examples.en: We would have traveled if we had had money.

27.tip: Think 'if this HAD happened, that WOULD HAVE happened' - both parts refer to the unreal past.
27.source_label: Using the past perfect subjunctive and the conditional perfect to express hypothetical situations

27.title: Verbs + EN + Infinitive
27.body: Some Spanish verbs require 'en' before an infinitive to express actions like insisting, persisting, or agreeing.

examples.en: I insist on paying the bill.

27.tip: Think 'EN' for verbs of mental focus: insist EN, agree EN, persist EN.

27.title: Verbs + DE + Infinitive
27.body: Some Spanish verbs require 'de' before a following infinitive verb. This structure often indicates separation, completion, or attempt.
27.tip: Think 'DE-tach' - verbs that detach from an action use 'de' + infinitive.

27.title: Verbo + A + Infinitivo
27.body: Some Spanish verbs require 'a' before an infinitive to connect the action to what follows.

examples.es: Ella se atreve a hablar en público.
examples.en: She dares to speak in public.

27.tip: Think of 'a' as a bridge linking the main verb to the action that follows.

27.title: Verbo + de + infinitivo
27.body: Some Spanish verbs require 'de' before an infinitive to express actions like remembering, stopping, or being glad about something.

examples.es: Me acuerdo de visitar a mi abuela.
Él dejó de fumar el año pasado.

27.tip: Think: 'de' connects the verb to the action—like a bridge to the infinitive.

27.title: Perfect Tenses Structure
27.body: All Spanish perfect tenses combine the appropriate tense of 'haber' with the past participle of the main verb.
27.tip: Think 'haber' + -ado/-ido = perfect tense

27.title: Diminutivos y Aumentativos
27.body: Suffixes like -ito/-ita (diminutive) and -ote/-ota (augmentative) modify nouns/adjectives to show size, affection, or sometimes negative tone.
27.tip: Drop the final vowel before adding the suffix: taza → tacita.

27.title: Present Subjunctive with Impersonal Expressions
27.body: Use the present subjunctive after impersonal expressions that show opinion, necessity, or uncertainty. These expressions often start with 'es' + adjective.

examples.es: Es importante que estudies para el examen.
examples.en: It's important that you study for the exam.
It's necessary that we arrive early.

27.tip: Think: If the expression shows someone's judgment about a situation (not a fact), trigger the subjunctive.

27.title: Subjuntivo con expresiones impersonales
27.body: Use the present subjunctive after impersonal expressions like 'es importante' or 'es necesario' when introducing a dependent clause with 'que' and a different subject.

examples.en: It's necessary that you arrive early.
It's good that we study together.

27.tip: If there's no 'que' and no subject change, use the infinitive instead (e.g., 'Es necesario estudiar').
27.source_label: ESTRUCTURAS ¡Atención! More uses of the present subjunctive: with impersonal expressions

27.title: Tal vez, quizá, ojalá, puede que
27.body: Use present subjunctive with these expressions when expressing uncertainty or hope. No 'que' needed with tal vez/quizá, optional with ojalá, required with puede que.
27.tip: Think 'maybe, hope, possibly' → subjunctive trigger.
27.source_label: ¡Atención! More uses of the present subjunctive: with tal vez, quizá(s), ojalá, and puede que

27.title: Apellidos españoles
