27.body: En español se usan dos apellidos: primero el paterno, luego el materno. En nombres con 'de', el apellido principal es el que viene antes.

examples.es: Blanca Rodríguez Peña → Rodríguez es el apellido paterno.
Blanca Rodríguez de Infante → Rodríguez es el apellido principal.

27.tip: Primero = padre, segundo = madre. 'De' señala un apellido compuesto.

27.title: Present Perfect vs Subjunctive
27.body: Use present perfect for completed actions (ha comprado). Use present perfect subjunctive after expressions of doubt/emotion (espero que hayan instalado).
27.tip: Subjunctive follows 'que' with emotion/doubt/wishes.
27.source_label: Present perfect / Present perfect subjunctive examples

27.title: Past Perfect vs Past Perfect Subjunctive
27.body: Use past perfect for completed past actions. Use past perfect subjunctive after expressions of doubt or emotion in the past.

examples.en: They had already used the computers.

examples.es: No creía que las hubieran usado.
examples.en: I didn't think they had used them.

27.tip: Think 'had done' vs 'might have done' - subjunctive adds uncertainty.
27.source_label: Past perfect / Past perfect subjunctive examples

27.title: Como si + Past Perfect Subjunctive
27.body: Use the past perfect subjunctive after 'como si' to describe hypothetical past situations, regardless of the main verb's tense.

examples.es: Habla de Chile como si hubiera vivido allí.
examples.en: He talks about Chile as if he had lived there.

examples.es: Actúa como si no hubiera pasado nada.
examples.en: He acts as if nothing had happened.

27.tip: Think 'como si' + 'had' in English (hubiera/hubiese + past participle).
27.source_label: ADDITIONAL INFORMATION: Past perfect subjunctive with como si

27.title: Subjuntivo en cláusulas adjetivas
27.body: Usa el subjuntivo cuando el antecedente (persona/cosa/lugar) no existe o es indefinido. Usa el indicativo cuando existe y es definido.

examples.es: Busco un libro que sea interesante.
examples.en: I'm looking for a book that is interesting (any interesting book).

examples.es: No hay nadie que hable quechua aquí.
examples.en: There isn't anyone who speaks Quechua here.

27.tip: ¿Indefinido o negativo? → Subjuntivo. ¿Definido y existente? → Indicativo.
27.source_label: ¡Atención! More uses of the present subjunctive: with adjectival clauses

27.title: Past Perfect Subjunctive + Conditional Perfect
27.body: Use these together to talk about hypothetical past situations and their imagined results. The past perfect subjunctive sets up the 'if' condition, and the conditional perfect describes what would have happened.

examples.es: Si hubieras estudiado, habrías aprobado el examen.
examples.en: If you had studied, you would have passed the exam.

examples.es: Si ellos me hubieran invitado, habría ido a la fiesta.
examples.en: If they had invited me, I would have gone to the party.

27.tip: Think 'If this HAD happened (hubiera), then that WOULD HAVE happened (habría).'

27.title: Past Perfect Subjunctive + Conditional Perfect
27.body: Use this structure for hypothetical past situations that didn't happen. The 'if' clause uses past perfect subjunctive, and the result uses conditional perfect.

examples.en: If I had studied, I would have passed.

examples.es: Habríamos viajado si hubiéramos tenido dinero.
examples.en: We would have traveled if we had had money.

27.tip: Think 'if this HAD happened, that WOULD HAVE happened' - both parts are in the past.
27.source_label: ¡Atención! Using the past perfect subjunctive and the conditional perfect to express hypothetical situations

28.title: Subjunctive with Doubt/Denial
28.body: Use the present subjunctive after expressions of doubt or denial like 'dudar que' or 'no creer que' when the speaker views the action as uncertain or untrue.

examples.es: Dudo que ella venga a la fiesta.
examples.en: I doubt she will come to the party.

28.tip: Think: If you doubt or deny it, use subjunctive.
28.source_label: More uses of the present subjunctive: with expressions of doubt and denial

28.title: Sequence of Tenses: Subjunctive
28.body: Use present subjunctive after non-past main verbs (present, future, commands). Use imperfect subjunctive after past main verbs (preterite, imperfect, conditional).

examples.es: El sargento ordena que el soldado estudie.
examples.en: The sergeant orders that the soldier study.

examples.es: El sargento ordenó que el soldado estudiara.
examples.en: The sergeant ordered that the soldier study.

28.tip: Present for present/future, imperfect for past.
28.source_label: Sequence of tenses using the present subjunctive and the imperfect subjunctive

28.title: Preterite vs Imperfect: Meaning Changes
28.body: Some verbs change meaning in preterite vs imperfect. Poder means 'managed to' (success) in preterite but 'was able to' (general ability) in imperfect.

examples.en: Yesterday I managed to go to the movies (and succeeded).
Before, I was able to go to the movies (general ability).

28.tip: Preterite = single event success/failure; Imperfect = ongoing ability/state.
28.source_label: LECCION 34 ¡Atención! Other verbs that change meaning when used in the preterite

28.title: Haber, Ser, Estar - Present Participles
28.body: These three verbs have irregular present participles: habiendo (having), siendo (being), and estando (being). They're used in compound tenses and absolute constructions.

examples.en: Being honest, I don't like it.

28.source_label: ¡Atención! The present participle of the verbs haber, ser, and estar

28.title: Verbs + EN + Infinitive
28.body: Some Spanish verbs require 'en' before an infinitive to express actions like persisting, agreeing, or delaying.

examples.en: I persist in studying every day.

28.tip: Think of 'en' as 'in' or 'on' connecting the verb to the action that follows.

28.title: Refranes
28.body: Refranes are Spanish proverbs—short, complete sayings that convey advice, wisdom, or life lessons through metaphorical language.

examples.en: A word to the wise is enough.

examples.es: Al que madruga, Dios lo ayuda.
examples.en: The early bird catches the worm.

28.tip: Think of refranes as cultural 'life hacks' passed down through generations—they often rhyme or use vivid imagery.

28.title: Cuando + Subjunctive
28.body: Use the present subjunctive after 'cuando' when the main clause refers to a future possibility. Use indicative for facts, habits, or past events.

examples.es: Te llamaré cuando llegue a casa.
examples.en: I'll call you when I get home.

examples.es: Siempre estudio cuando hace buen tiempo.
examples.en: I always study when the weather is nice.

28.tip: Subjunctive for future 'when', indicative for regular 'when'.
28.source_label: ¡Atención! More uses of the present subjunctive: with cuando

28.title: Sequence of Tenses
28.body: Use present subjunctive after present/future main verbs, and imperfect subjunctive after past main verbs.
28.tip: Present with present, past with past.
28.source_label: ¡Atención! Sequence of tenses using the present subjunctive and the imperfect subjunctive

28.title: Como si + Imperfect Subjunctive
28.body: Use 'como si' to describe hypothetical comparisons, always followed by imperfect subjunctive.

examples.es: Habla como si supiera la verdad.
examples.en: He talks as if he knew the truth.
She runs as if she were in a hurry.

28.tip: Think 'as if' → always triggers imperfect subjunctive in Spanish.

28.title: Subjuntivo con duda/negación
28.body: Use present subjunctive after expressions of doubt or denial like 'dudar que', 'negar que', or 'no creer que' in the main clause.

examples.es: Dudo que ella venga a la fiesta.
examples.en: I doubt she will come to the party.

28.tip: Think: If the main clause expresses uncertainty or denial, the dependent clause needs subjunctive.
28.source_label: ESTRUCTURAS ¡Atención! More uses of the present subjunctive: with expressions of doubt and denial

28.title: Construir vs. Extraer
28.body: Use 'construir' for building or creating something tangible or abstract, and 'extraer' for extracting or obtaining information from a source.

examples.es: Vamos a construir el perfil de un buen líder.
examples.en: We are going to construct the profile of a good leader.

examples.es: Debemos extraer información de las noticias.
examples.en: We must extract information from the news.

28.tip: Think: construir = construct/build, extraer = extract/pull out.

28.title: Verbs with Preterite Meaning Shifts
28.body: Some verbs change meaning in the preterite, often indicating a single completed attempt or result rather than a general state.

examples.en: I failed to open the door (tried).
I tried to help you yesterday.

28.tip: Preterite = punctual attempt/result; Imperfect = general ability/state.
28.source_label: Other verbs that change meaning when used in the preterite

28.title: Como si + Subjunctive
28.body: Use 'como si' (as if) with imperfect subjunctive for present/unreal comparisons, or past perfect subjunctive for past/unreal comparisons.

examples.en: He talks as if he were rich.

examples.es: Actuaba como si lo hubiera planeado.
examples.en: He acted as if he had planned it.

28.tip: Think: 'como si' always triggers subjunctive—past for present, double-past for past.
28.source_label: Using the expression como si... (as if...)

28.title: Gerundio Compuesto vs Simple
28.body: Use the compound gerund (habiendo + participle) for actions completed before another action. Use the simple gerund (estando/siendo) for simultaneous actions or states.

examples.es: Habiendo comprado su boleto, él canceló su viaje.
examples.en: Having bought his ticket, he canceled his trip.

examples.es: Estando de vacaciones, él conoció a su esposa.
examples.en: While on vacation, he met his wife.

28.tip: Habiendo = 'having done' (completed action); Estando/Siendo = 'while being' (ongoing state).

29.title: Subjunctive with Emotions
29.body: Use present subjunctive after emotion verbs like alegrarse, sentir, temer when there's a different subject in the dependent clause introduced by 'que'.

examples.es: Temen que los guerrilleros ataquen otra vez.
examples.en: They fear that the guerrillas will attack again.

examples.es: Me alegro de que estés aquí.

29.tip: No subjunctive needed if same subject - use infinitive instead: 'Tengo miedo de ir' (I'm afraid to go).
29.source_label: Culmulative Grammar Notes: More uses of the present subjunctive: with verbs that express emotion

29.title: Subjuntivo con emociones
29.body: Usa el subjuntivo después de verbos de emoción (alegrarse, temer, sentir) cuando hay sujetos diferentes, introducido por 'que'.

examples.es: Temen que los guerrilleros ataquen otra vez.
examples.en: They fear that the guerrillas will attack again.

examples.es: Me alegro de que estés aquí.
examples.en: I'm happy that you are here.

29.tip: ¿Dos personas? → subjuntivo. Una persona? → infinitivo (ej: 'Temo viajar solo').

29.title: Subjuntivo en cláusulas adverbiales
29.body: Usa el subjuntivo con conjunciones como 'a menos que', 'para que' y 'sin que'. Con 'en cuanto' o 'hasta que', usa indicativo para hechos y subjuntivo para futuro.

examples.en: I will study until you arrive.

examples.es: Lo haré a menos que me lo prohíbas.
examples.en: I'll do it unless you forbid me.

29.tip: ¿Es seguro? → indicativo. ¿Es incierto o futuro? → subjuntivo.
29.source_label: ¡Atención! More uses of the present subjunctive: with adverbial clauses
